posts.summary: Cantrill introduces himself and explains the fundamental problem that led to DTrace: software is invisible during execution, and any attempt to observe it slows it down. This creates a paradox where debug builds that can be inspected are too slow for production, forcing engineers to reproduce symptoms in development environments—which often leads to misdiagnosis because different root causes can manifest identical symptoms. DTrace solves this by enabling dynamic instrumentation of optimized production systems without requiring code changes or restarts.
key_points: Traditional debugging requires choosing between fast production code you can't see and slow debug code you can observe
Reproducing production problems in development is fundamentally flawed because you're reproducing symptoms, not root causes
Disjoint problems can manifest the same symptoms, leading to incorrect diagnoses
DTrace enables dynamic instrumentation of production systems that is absolutely safe
money_quotes: Above all else, I believe that software should: Work correctly. Work efficiently.
The fundamental difficulty is that we cannot see software.
Software has the peculiar property that to see it, you must make it slower.
When you're reproducing performance problems, you're reproducing symptoms.
Disjoint problems can manifest the same symptoms.
How many times have you found 'a' problem without finding 'the' problem?
posts.key_insight: The inability to observe production software without degrading its performance forces engineers into a diagnostic trap where they reproduce symptoms rather than root causes, making DTrace's zero-overhead dynamic instrumentation a paradigm shift.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces an upcoming online Expert Exchange chat session about DTrace, featuring himself and fellow DTrace creators Adam Leventhal and Mike Shapiro. The brief post invites readers to participate and get their questions answered directly by the people who wrote the code.
key_points: DTrace Expert Exchange scheduled for Wednesday at 10am Pacific
Features all three DTrace creators: Cantrill, Leventhal, and Shapiro
Direct Q&A opportunity with the actual code authors
money_quotes: This is your opportunity to have your questions answered on-line by the people who wrote the code. (Which is to say, us.)
posts.key_insight: The DTrace team valued direct engagement with their user community, offering real-time access to the creators themselves.

posts.summary: Cantrill recounts a minor technical mishap during a live DTrace Expert Exchange chat session where his laptop's 'y' key stopped working mid-session. He improvised by first trying to paste the letter from a buffer, then physically prying off the key to press the underlying mechanism directly. The post serves as a brief follow-up to thank participants and direct them to the DTrace forum for further questions.
key_points: Hosted a live Expert Exchange session about DTrace with Adam
Keyboard malfunction required creative improvisation mid-chat
The letter 'y' appears frequently enough that working around its absence proved nearly impossible
Directed users to the DTrace forum for ongoing questions
money_quotes: Like many of the letters that you wouldn't pick first on Wheel of Fortune – y's frequency is still sufficiently high to make living without it nearly impossible.

So I took out my car keys, pried off my 'y' key, took off the hard plastic mechanism underneath it, and discovered that I could still get a y if I prodded the soft plastic underbelly of what used to be my 'y' key.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most memorable technical challenges aren't in the code but in the physical tools we use to write it.

posts.summary: Cantrill expresses frustration that press coverage of a technical roundtable focused almost entirely on open source speculation despite it being a tiny fraction of the actual discussion. He clarifies a misquoted comment about disparaging code comments, explaining the technical context behind his frustration with C unions in the Solaris debugger. The post reflects the tension between wanting to discuss substantive technical work and the media's fixation on business strategy narratives.
key_points: Press coverage fixated on open source despite being less than 0.1% of roundtable content
Clarification that the disparaging comment was about C unions, not C++
The comment relates to postmortem object type identification in mdb debugger
Unions make type identification needlessly difficult in crash dump analysis
money_quotes: Again, despite the fact that it was less than one tenth of one percent of the content of the rountable, the headline is open source.
Note: C, not C++ – not that C++ unions don't deserve the same coarse words, only that C++ has bigger problems than just unions.
The presence of unions makes type identification needlessly difficult.
I'm still holding out some hope that we'll see an article on the actual technical content that we presented, and not the open sourcing of Solaris that we refused to talk about…
posts.key_insight: Technical practitioners often find that media coverage prioritizes business narratives over the substantive engineering work they actually want to discuss.

posts.summary: Cantrill recounts a press roundtable about Solaris 10's major technologies (DTrace, Zones, ZFS) that was overshadowed by journalists' interest in the open source announcement. Despite spending the entire session on technical deep-dives and explicitly declining to discuss open sourcing details, the resulting press coverage focused almost entirely on open source. He also corrects misquotations, noting the difference between saying something is 'technically not a problem' versus 'not a technical problem.'
key_points: Press coverage of technical briefings often fixates on business/strategy angles over the actual technology
Open sourcing Solaris was 'not a technical problem' - implying the challenges were legal, business, or political
Sun engineers had pride in their code quality and were eager to share it with the world
Journalists tend to paraphrase in ways that subtly change meaning
money_quotes: This is not a technical problem.
We're engineers; we obviously understand the value of having the source code. We believe that we have some of the cleanest code anywhere, and we're looking forward to showing it to the world.
We do have some of the cleanest code anywhere – but somehow I don't think that we're going to see a story about the beautiful ASCII art block comments in /usr/include/sys/dtrace_impl.h…
posts.key_insight: When engineers try to discuss technology with the press, business narratives often dominate the coverage regardless of what was actually discussed.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill celebrates his DTrace colleague Adam Leventhal joining Sun's blogging community. He playfully notes that convincing their teammate Mike to blog would be as difficult as getting the A-Team's B.A. Baracus to fly. The brief post reflects the early days of developer blogging at Sun Microsystems.
key_points: Adam Leventhal has started blogging at Sun
Mike remains resistant to blogging
Team DTrace aims for 'maximal blogging power'
money_quotes: Now if we can only convince Mike to start a blog (a feat that can only be compared to getting B.A. Baracus to fly), Team DTrace will be at maximal blogging power…
posts.key_insight: Developer evangelism through blogging was becoming an important part of open source project promotion in the mid-2000s.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on the essential role of early adopters in birthing revolutionary technologies, arguing that 'God bless the early adopters' should be Silicon Valley's true motto. He describes early adopters as people who endure pain, tedium, and the slow march toward usability while somehow maintaining enthusiasm for nascent technology. The post celebrates Jon Haslam as a canonical DTrace early adopter who advocated for the technology even in its unfinished state.
key_points: Early adopters are essential to birthing revolutionary technology
They endure tremendous pain and tedium while remaining enthusiastic advocates
DTrace's success depended on early adopters like Jon Haslam
Sturgeon's Law applies universally: 95% of everything is crap
money_quotes: For it is the Early Adopter who puts up with tremendous pain to get their hands on a technology, goes through the tedium of constantly communicating the technology's shortcomings to its inventors, endures the slow march towards something usable, and through it all somehow finds the energy to talk enthusiastically about the nascent technology at every opportunity.
The Early Adopters are something of a riddle to me, but they're so incredibly important to birthing new technology, that I almost view it as uncouth to dissect what makes them tick.
If you have ever developed a revolutionary technology – one that requires people to change the way they think at some level – you know how unbelievably true this is.
95% of everything is crap.
posts.key_insight: Revolutionary technologies depend not just on their inventors but on the mysterious, essential early adopters who endure the painful journey from prototype to product while evangelizing the technology's potential.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on media coverage of a Solaris 10 press roundtable, noting that while journalists initially wrote only about open source (a topic not discussed), a new article finally covered the actual technologies: DTrace, Zones, and ZFS. However, he expresses frustration that while the article gets the big picture right about DTrace's capabilities, the specific technical example cited is bizarrely inaccurate, leading to concerns about how he'll be perceived given previous misquotes.
key_points: Media coverage of technical topics often misses the mark on details even when the overall message is correct
DTrace enables visibility into system problems that were previously invisible
Technical misquotes can lead to unfair characterizations of engineers
Getting press attention for the right topics doesn't guarantee accurate reporting
money_quotes: DTrace, which uses more than 30,000 data monitoring points in the kernel alone, lets administrators see their entire system in a new way, revealing systemic problems that were previously invisible and fixing performance issues that used to go unresolved.

The details of the specific example are incredibly wrong. (So wrong, in fact, that they're just odd; what does 'a wild-card desktop applet that had somehow gotten channeled into the central system' even mean?)
Perhaps the terms used are so opaque that readers will come away confused, but with the right overall impression.
posts.key_insight: Getting media coverage for technical work is a double-edged sword: you might finally get attention for the right topics, but the technical details will likely be mangled in ways that range from confusing to embarrassing.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on a small victory when a tech journalist corrected factual errors in a Solaris 10 story after Cantrill complained on his blog. He uses the experience to consider the difficult position of technical reporters who must translate complex information from fast-talking engineers while readers expect perfect accuracy. The post acknowledges both sides of the accuracy problem: engineers who dump technical detail and expect perfection, and readers who blame technology rather than journalism when details are wrong.
key_points: Complaining publicly on a blog led to a journalist correcting technical errors
Technical reporters face the challenge of dealing with fast-talking engineers who expect absolute accuracy
Readers assume technical articles are accurate and blame the technology or technologist when details are wrong
There's a tension between the difficulty of technical reporting and the consequences of inaccuracy
money_quotes: It must take a thick skin to be dealing with people like me all of the time: fast talkers who bombard with technical detail, and then expect absolute accuracy in whatever stories emerge.

The problem is that the readership assumes absolute accuracy – and if the technical details are incorrect, they will naturally blame the technology (or worse, the technologist) instead of questioning the accuracy of the reportage.
posts.key_insight: Technical inaccuracy in journalism creates an unfair attribution problem where the technologist, not the reporter, bears the reputational cost of errors.

posts.summary: Cantrill recounts presenting DTrace at USENIX '04 and being asked by an IBM employee when it would be ported to Linux. He argues against the prevailing view that operating systems are commodities, asserting instead that the OS is a 'nexus of innovation.' He maintains that Solaris 10's technologies like DTrace, Zones, and ZFS demonstrate this innovation, and users who want these features should run Solaris rather than expect ports to Linux.
key_points: DTrace paper presentation at USENIX '04 was well-received
IBM employee asked when DTrace would be ported to Linux
Many believe operating systems are commodities - all basically the same
Sun disagrees: the operating system is a nexus of innovation
Solaris 10 technologies (DTrace, Zones, ZFS) prove OS innovation is possible
If you want the innovation, run Solaris - don't expect Linux ports
money_quotes: We believe in choice; we believe that people should pick the best operating system for the task at hand.
Many seem to believe that the operating system is a 'commodity' – that all operating systems are basically the same. We disagree: we believe that the operating system is a nexus of innovation.
Please stop asking if we're going to port these features to Linux – if you want to take advantage of our OS innovation, run Solaris.
posts.key_insight: The operating system is not a commodity but a nexus of innovation, and companies that invest in OS-level innovation should use that differentiation as a competitive advantage rather than giving it away to competing platforms.

posts.summary: Cantrill recaps a well-attended Solaris BOF (Birds of a Feather) session at USENIX '04, where he discussed DTrace, Zones, and other upcoming Solaris 10 technologies. He shares resources for attendees and expresses gratitude for the positive reception. The post hints at his playful disdain for compiler developers and teases upcoming features like ZFS.
key_points: Solaris BOF at USENIX '04 was well-attended
Discussed DTrace, Zones, and Predictive self-healing
ZFS and service management facility coming soon
Positive community reception to Solaris 10 technologies
money_quotes: It's tremendously rewarding for us to see you so excited about these technologies!
posts.key_insight: The Solaris team found genuine motivation in seeing the developer community embrace their new technologies at USENIX.

posts.summary: Cantrill reviews two books about beating casino odds: 'Bringing Down the House' about MIT card counters and 'The Eudaemonic Pie' about physicists building a device to predict roulette. He finds the latter more compelling because the technical problem is harder and the protagonists more intellectually serious. The post is a light book recommendation that reveals his preference for rigorous technical challenges over clever schemes.
key_points: Compares two books about beating casino games through analytical methods
Prefers 'The Eudaemonic Pie' for its harder technical problem (predicting roulette via physics)
Contrasts the life trajectories of the protagonists from each book
Criticizes 'Bringing Down the House' for the author's self-insertion into the narrative
money_quotes: The book was brain candy in the purest sense: it was exhilerating and fun – but it definitely ruined my dinner.

Bass's subjects are more interesting to me, if only because the problem they're solving is so much harder: a group of physicists and computer scientists develop a device to give them an advantage over roulette. (After all, it's just Newtonian physics, right?)

And while the 'Bringing Down the House' protagonists seem destined for a life of overpaid corporate consulting and/or 12 step programs, the leader of the 'Eudaemonic' tribe, Doyne Farmer, now writes papers for academic journals.
I didn't drop fifteen bones to read 'The Making of Bringing Down the House'…
posts.key_insight: The harder the technical problem, the more intellectually satisfying and ultimately meaningful the pursuit—a preference that distinguishes serious technologists from clever opportunists.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that USENIX has become disconnected from its mission of supporting research with a 'practical bias,' with the General Session dominated by academic papers that solve problems too small to be relevant to industry practitioners. Drawing a parallel to mechanical engineering, he suggests that systems research has matured to the point where meaningful contributions require resources beyond what a single graduate student can provide, yet unlike other engineering fields, USENIX hasn't adapted by integrating industry practitioners into its program committees. He proposes eliminating the FREENIX track and folding industry voices into the main program to prevent the conference from becoming irrelevant.
key_points: DTrace paper was the only paper at USENIX '04 to come exclusively from industry
Academic systems research solves problems 'sufficiently small to be solvable by a single graduate student'
Engineering disciplines like automotive stopped academic programs when contributing to state-of-the-art became too expensive for academia alone
USENIX '05 Program Committee has inverse ratio of academia to industry compared to traditional engineering conferences
FREENIX track risks becoming a 'dumping ground' for practically-biased work, separating academics from practitioners
money_quotes: The content of the General Session was thus academic in the strictest sense: it consisted of papers written by graduate students, solving problems in systems sufficiently small to be solvable by a single graduate student working for a reasonably short period of time.
The problem is that many of these systems – to me at least – are so small as to not be terribly relevant.
These programs were stopped because the economics no longer made sense: it became prohibitively expensive to meaningfully contribute to the state-of-the-art.
The disciplines were mature enough that there was an established industry that understood that research begat differentiated product, and differentiated product begat profit.

Having the General Session become strictly academic and leaving the FREENIX track to become strictly industrial effectively separates the academics from the practitioners. And this, in my opinion, is exactly what we don't need.
How do we prevent USENIX from becoming irrelevant?
posts.key_insight: Systems research has matured to the point where meaningful contributions require industry-scale resources, and academic conferences must integrate practitioners or risk irrelevance.

posts.summary: In this brief, humorous post, Cantrill responds to being compared to infomercial pitchman Ron Popeil by The Register. He playfully 'clarifies' that DTrace cannot make turkey-jerky and denies rumors of a DTrace infomercial, while jokingly playing along with the promotional comparison.
key_points: The Register compared Cantrill's DTrace evangelism to Ron Popeil's pitchman style
Cantrill responds with self-deprecating humor rather than defensiveness
The post demonstrates Cantrill's willingness to engage with media characterizations playfully
money_quotes: Despite some claims to the contrary, DTrace cannot be used to make turkey-jerky.
It is true that many have used DTrace to work their way up from lowly busboys to yacht-owning multi-millionaires…
posts.key_insight: Cantrill embraces rather than resists being characterized as an enthusiastic promoter of DTrace, using humor to acknowledge his passionate advocacy for the technology.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that USENIX's declining industry participation on Program Committees threatens its mission of fostering research with a 'practical bias.' He presents data showing industry representation dropped from roughly half in 1996 to a minority by 2005, with product-oriented engineers virtually disappearing. Rather than placing blame solely on industry or academia, he argues USENIX itself must actively seek out and convince industry practitioners to participate, and proposes the USENIX Board charter a joint committee to address the problem.
key_points: Industry participation on USENIX Program Committees has declined dramatically over nine years
The responsibility for keeping USENIX relevant lies with USENIX itself, not just industry or academia
USENIX must actively seek out industry participants rather than waiting for volunteers
A joint industry-academia committee should be chartered to develop concrete recommendations
money_quotes: The responsibility for keeping USENIX relevant doesn't lie exclusively with industry – and it doesn't lie exclusively with academia, either. Rather, the responsibility lies with USENIX itself, for it is the mission of USENIX to encourage research with a 'practical bias.'

USENIX cannot simply wait for volunteers from industry to materialize – USENIX must seek out people in industry who understand both the academic and the industrial sides of systems research, and they must convince these people to work on a Program Committee.
An organization that has dedicated itself to research with a 'practical bias' is clearly losing that bias in its flagship conference.
If we can find more like him from both industry and academia, we can get the 'practical bias' back into USENIX.
posts.key_insight: Organizations dedicated to bridging industry and academia must take active responsibility for maintaining that bridge, rather than passively waiting for participation from either side.

posts.summary: Cantrill responds to Rob Pike's argument that systems software research has become irrelevant, agreeing with the general diagnosis but challenging Pike's narrow definition of what constitutes meaningful systems work. He argues that systems research should focus on the hidden internals of systems rather than just interfaces, and that valuable innovation can come in smaller, more focused forms. He concludes by positioning open source Solaris as a platform that could enable more relevant academic systems research by providing researchers access to a modern, production-quality system.
key_points: Systems research should focus on internal mechanisms, not just system interfaces
Innovation need not be OS-scale to be meaningful - debuggers, file systems, and virtualization count
Even smaller problems are too large for typical academic timelines
Small, focused work can be innovative if it's robust and thorough
Open source Solaris aims to give researchers a modern foundation to build upon
money_quotes: In my opinion, 'systems research' should focus not on the interface of the system, but rather its guts: those hidden Rube Goldberg-esque innards that are rife with old assumptions and unintended consequences.
Understanding phenomena is a prerequisite to understanding larger systemic truths.
A new file system, a new debugger, or a new way of virtualization can be just as exciting.
Smaller work can be interesting – as long as it's innovative, robust and thorough.
We wish to provide complete access to a best-of-breed system that allows researchers to solve new problems instead of revisiting old ones.
posts.key_insight: Meaningful systems research doesn't require building entire operating systems; smaller, deeply understood innovations in system internals can be just as valuable if they're thorough and address real phenomena.

posts.summary: Cantrill responds to a Linux kernel mailing list post where Karim Yaghmour claimed that LTT and DProbes provide essentially the same capabilities as DTrace. Cantrill argues this claim is false, pointing out that DTrace has fundamental architectural features like aggregations and thread-local variables that LTT lacks entirely. He also acknowledges the valid frustration that valuable projects like LTT and LKCD have been denied entry into the Linux kernel for seemingly non-technical reasons.
key_points: LTT and DProbes lack core DTrace features like aggregations and thread-local variables
These missing features are architectural, not mere syntactic sugar
Linux kernel governance has blocked valuable projects for seemingly petty reasons
A RedHat developer's independent comparison reached similar conclusions about DTrace's superiority
money_quotes: For all of its putative development speed, Linux has a surprising number of otherwise valuable projects that have been repeatedly denied entry for reasons that seem to be petty and non-technical.
This claim is false, and indicates that while Karim may have scanned The Register article, he didn't bother to browse even our USENIX paper – let alone our documentation.
Two among these are aggregations and thread-local variables – two features that are not syntactic sugar or bolted-on afterthoughts, but rather are core to the DTrace architecture.
These features turn out to be essential in using DTrace to quickly resolve problems.
posts.key_insight: Surface-level feature comparisons between tracing tools miss the fundamental architectural differences that make DTrace's aggregations and thread-local variables essential for real-world debugging.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill responds to claims from the Linux Trace Toolkit (LTT) developers that DTrace's success was primarily due to favorable integration circumstances at Sun, rather than technical merit. He clarifies that DTrace actually spent two years outside the Solaris gate, built a substantial user community during that time, and integrated most of its features in a single large putback. Cantrill argues that LTT's roadmap from 2002 shows no evidence they were planning DTrace's fundamental innovations like aggregations, speculative tracing, or thread-local variables.
key_points: DTrace integrated most features in a single putback on September 3, 2003, contrary to claims it was built incrementally
DTrace spent two years outside the Solaris gate, developing and building a user community before integration
By integration time, DTrace had hundreds of internal users and a proven track record of solving real problems
LTT's 2002 roadmap contained no plans for DTrace fundamentals like aggregations, speculative tracing, or associative arrays
The Solaris model expects integrated projects to be complete and usable from a customer's perspective
money_quotes: In Solaris, projects that integrate are expected to be in a completed state; if there is follow-on work planned, fine – but what integrates into the gate must be something that is usable and complete from a customer's perspective.
We didn't let the fact that we had not yet integrated prevent us from developing DTrace, nor did we let it prevent us from building a user community around DTrace.

I'm getting a little tired of this argument that LTT would be exactly where DTrace is had they only been allowed into the Linux kernel five years ago. I believe that there is some fundamental innovation in DTrace that LTT simply did not anticipate.

In that document, you will find many future directions, but not so much as a whisper of the need for DTrace basics like aggregations, speculative tracing, thread-local variables or associative arrays.

Would LTT be further along now had it been allowed to integrate into Linux five years ago? Surely. Would it be anywhere near where DTrace is today? In the immortal words of the Magic 8-Ball, 'very doubtful.'
posts.key_insight: Technical innovation and building a user community matter more than early integration access—DTrace's capabilities weren't the result of favorable circumstances but of fundamental design innovations that competitors simply hadn't conceived.

posts.summary: Cantrill recounts experiencing a demo failure when DTrace crashed in front of an important customer audience at a Fortune 100 company. Despite the humiliation, he managed to diagnose the problem was specific to one program, continued with the demo successfully, and fixed the bug on his flight home. He defends his practice of always demoing on the latest unreleased builds, arguing that finding bugs through personal embarrassment ultimately leads to a better product.
key_points: DTrace offers unusually compelling demos for systems software, which typically has boring demonstrations
The 'FCS Quality All the Time' philosophy means always running and demoing the latest development builds
Demo failures create an immediate visceral reaction combining shock, humiliation, and fight-or-flight response
When demos fail, the professional response is to diagnose the scope of the problem and continue if possible
Running latest bits in demos helps find bugs before customers do, improving final product quality
money_quotes: If you've never had it, there's no feeling quite like having a demo blow up on you: it's as if you peed your pants, failed an exam and were punched in the gut – all at the same horrifying instant.
In the time it takes a single process to dump core, you go from an (over)confident technologist to a frightened woodland creature, transfixed by the light of an oncoming freight train.

Our mantra in Solaris Kernel Development is 'FCS Quality All the Time' – we believe that the product should always be ready to be run in production. And if we're going to tell a customer that it's ready to be run in production, we damn well better run it in production ourselves.
So who am I to get upset about a little personal humiliation if the upshot is a better product?
posts.key_insight: Personal embarrassment from demo failures is a worthwhile price for the quality improvements that come from dogfooding the latest development builds.

posts.summary: Cantrill walks through a comprehensive DTrace demonstration, starting from basic probe listing and system call tracing, then progressively drilling down to identify why dhcpagent repeatedly opens its config file. The demo showcases DTrace's power to aggregate data in-situ rather than post-mortem, instrument running applications without modification, and trace execution flow seamlessly across user-kernel boundaries. The post serves as both a practical tutorial and a showcase of how DTrace enables asking and answering questions iteratively, like a good conversation.
key_points: DTrace can instrument over 30,000 probes on a stock system, with the pid provider able to instrument every instruction in every running app
In-situ data aggregation is a fundamental DTrace innovation that avoids the probe effect of streaming raw data
The demo follows a conversational pattern where each answer provokes the next question
DTrace can trace execution flow across the user/kernel boundary in a single unified view
Instrumentation is dynamic - apps return to their optimized state when tracing stops
money_quotes: Indeed, the best demos are when someone in the audience shouts out their own question that they want to see answered: answering such a question instantly with DTrace nearly always blows away the questioner – who has presumably suffered in the past trying to answer similar questions.
In DTrace – as in good cocktail conversation – the answer to one question typically provokes the next question.

This is a serious problem, and the DTrace solution represents a substantial difference from virtually all earlier work in this domain: in DTrace, the aggregation of data is a first-class citizen. Instead of aggregating data postmortem with traditional Unix tools, DTrace allows data to be aggregated in situ.
At the risk of sounding too much like Ron Popeil, the above output is the clearest display of code flow across a protection boundary from any tool – research or otherwise.
Once it's fixed, I will sadly have to find some other sub-optimal software with which to demonstrate DTrace. The good news is that such software isn't terribly hard to find.
posts.tone: instructional, enthusiastic, technically detailed with dry humor
posts.key_insight: The most powerful debugging tool is one that lets you ask questions iteratively and get answers immediately, turning system investigation into a conversation rather than a batch process.

posts.summary: Cantrill responds to a Linux kernel mailing list discussion about DTrace, where a poster dismissively characterized it as mere 'profiling' without doing basic research. He points out that the critic could have easily found extensive technical documentation by simply Googling 'dtrace' or reading the footnotes of the article he criticized. Cantrill frames this as an example of Linux community bigotry where preconceived notions trump actually understanding new technology.
key_points: A Linux kernel mailing list poster dismissed DTrace as overhyped 'profiling' without researching what it actually does
The critic failed to perform basic due diligence like Googling 'dtrace' or reading article footnotes with technical details
DTrace's key differentiator is arbitrary and safe system instrumentation, not just profiling
Even sympathetic Linux community responses missed DTrace's broader capabilities
money_quotes: Yes, you missed something Julien: you forgot to type 'dtrace' into google.
Sometimes the bigotry surrounding Linux suprises even me: in the time he took to record his misconceptions, Julien could have (easily!) figured out that he was completely wrong.
But I guess that even this is too much work for someone who is looking to confirm preconceived notions rather than understand new technology…
One can instrument one's system arbitrarily and safely with DTrace – but at least he correctly identifies one capacity in which DTrace clearly leads the pack.
posts.key_insight: Technical tribalism leads people to dismiss innovations from rival ecosystems without even basic research, prioritizing confirmation of prejudices over understanding new technology.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that software's unique economics—zero variable costs but high fixed costs—combined with its non-commodity nature creates perverse incentives where vendors exploit customer lock-in rather than compete on merit. He introduces the concept of the 'FYO point' (the threshold at which customers become so frustrated they reevaluate their software decisions) and explains how both demand-side and supply-side forces drive the inevitable rise of open source. The piece predicts that markets like databases will eventually follow operating systems toward open source, though the timeline remains uncertain.
key_points: Software has zero variable costs but high fixed costs, creating unusual market dynamics
Customer demand becomes increasingly price inelastic over time due to dependencies and switching costs
Vendors focus on raising barriers to exit rather than competing, which Cantrill finds 'abhorrent'
Open source emerges from both demand-side (customer frustration) and supply-side (competitive strategy) forces
Supply-side open source from professional developers ultimately produces higher quality software
The open sourcing of Solaris represents 'open source as counter-strike' rather than capitulation
money_quotes: Software is like nothing else in the history of human endeavor: unlike everything else we have ever built, software costs nothing to manufacture, and it never wears out.

Your friendly software vendor knows that your demand tends towards inelasticity – which is why they so frequently raise the rent while offering so little in return. We've always known about this demand inelasticity, we've just called it something else: vendor lock-in.

I now have had not one but two software vendors tell me that I must add a way to disable DTrace for their app to prevent their own customers from observing their software. They're not even worried about their competitors – they're too busy screwing their own customers!
To put this in retail terms, open source software has all of the properties of a loss-leader – minus the loss, of course.
And unlike much prior supply-side open source activity, the open sourcing of Solaris is not open source as capitulation – it is open source as counter-strike.
posts.key_insight: Software's zero marginal cost and tendency toward customer lock-in makes open source an economic inevitability, driven by both frustrated customers and competitors seeking to lower each other's switching costs.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces the birth of his son Tobin Cormac Gaffikin Cantrill on September 9th, 2004. He reflects on how the experience of having a newborn humbles him as someone who has spent his career working with complex engineered systems. The post uses the occasion to take a jab at artificial intelligence research, noting that his newborn accomplished more in his first thirty minutes than AI has in 45 years.
key_points: Tobin was born September 9th, 2004 at 8:59am, weighing 8 pounds 11.5 ounces
The complexity of biological systems humbles engineers who work with man-made systems
Cantrill uses the birth to critique the hubris of strong AI research
He planned to return to work on October 4th with DTrace updates
money_quotes: Having a baby is a humbling experience for anyone, but it's especially so when you've spent most of your life dealing with complex man-made systems: it boggles the mind that a system so incredibly intricate could ever work at all, let alone so reliably.
Tobin accomplished more in his first thirty minutes of life than AI has accomplished in 45 years -- and he's certainly a hell of a lot cuter than your average AI lab rat.
posts.key_insight: The staggering complexity of biological systems like a newborn baby should humble engineers and serve as a reality check against the grandiose claims of artificial intelligence research.

posts.summary: Cantrill provides updates on DTrace's growing momentum, including Sun's DTrace Challenge contest with surprisingly valuable prizes, the launch of formal DTrace training courses, and positive press coverage in the New York Times. He expresses pride in Solaris 10 being recognized as a 'generational shift' in operating systems. The post captures a moment of optimism at Sun as DTrace and Solaris 10 gain traction in the industry.
key_points: Sun launched the DTrace Challenge with significant prizes including HDTVs and laptops
Formal DTrace training courses are now available
New York Times coverage positioned Solaris 10 as critical to Sun's future
Jonathan Adams, who did the DTrace code review, joined Sun's blogging community
money_quotes: When this idea was first floated internally, I assumed that the prizes would, well, kinda suck. Maybe a T-shirt or a mug or some Solaris DVDs or whatever.
I can say with confidence that there are bodies of in-kernel DTrace code that are understood by only two people – Jonathan and me.
Markoff nailed it: Solaris 10 is indeed a 'generational shift' – and it's one that we on Team DTrace are very much proud to be a part of…
Needless to say, I've never been so bummed to see the 'employees and their families are inelgible' rider – even at his young age, I'm sure Tobin could have developed a winning D script for his Da-da!
posts.key_insight: Solaris 10 and DTrace represented a genuine technological leap that Sun hoped would restore the company's competitive position against Linux and Windows.

posts.summary: Cantrill recounts the successful Solaris 10 launch, highlighting its emphasis on real technical substance and customer testimonials rather than marketing fluff. The event featured detailed technical presentations to journalists and a powerful endorsement from FedEx describing their wins with DTrace. He also addresses HP's competitive response, dismissing it as baseless FUD from a non-technologist attacking solved problems like endianness.
key_points: Solaris 10 launch emphasized technical depth and real customer impact over marketing hype
FedEx publicly vouched for DTrace on stage, a career highlight for Cantrill
HP's competitive response attacked non-issues like endianness rather than actual technology
Fifty journalists attended a two-hour technical boot camp showing genuine press interest
money_quotes: it was very important to us that those covering the event understand that this isn't ballyhooed nothingness – this is real technology that is having a tangible impact on those using it.
it's something else entirely to have a customer be willing to stand on a stage with you and put their reputation on the line by vouching for your technology.

And on top of it all, to have that customer be FedEx – a company that I (and most, I suspect) hold in very high regard – well, it nearly brought a tear to my eye; moments like that just don't come often in one's career…
this is pure HP FUD: it doesn't attack our technology in any concrete fashion, but rather attempts to put baseless fear in the minds of those who might be considering it.
But then, his attack reveals what is confirmed by Fink's bio (and photo?): Fink isn't a technologist.
posts.key_insight: The most meaningful validation for technology isn't press coverage or marketing success, but customers willing to stake their reputation on it publicly.

posts.summary: Cantrill shares an internal Sun Microsystems animated series called 'Inside Jack' that humorously promotes Solaris 10. He compares it favorably to South Park's irreverent style and notes it features an animated appearance by Mike Shapiro, the third and typically behind-the-scenes member of the DTrace team.
key_points: Sun created an animated series called 'Inside Jack' to promote Solaris 10
The series has a South Park-like irreverent humor style
Mike Shapiro, the 'shadowy' third member of Team DTrace, makes a rare animated appearance
money_quotes: Finally, we have the South Park of operating systems: Inside Jack.
It even offers a rare glimpse (albeit animated) at Mike, the shadowy and elusive third member of Team DTrace.
posts.key_insight: Sun Microsystems used irreverent, South Park-style animation as an unconventional marketing approach for Solaris 10, reflecting the playful culture within the engineering teams.

posts.summary: Cantrill recounts his experience running VENIX 2.0, a 1984 UNIX variant, on a DEC Pro 350 simulator. He marvels at how recognizable the basic tools remain after twenty years—vi, cc, and adb all feel familiar. Yet he's equally struck by how primitive the system is compared to modern operating systems, lacking TCP/IP, real virtual memory, and proper observability, which highlights how far systems software has advanced.
key_points: VENIX 2.0 ran easily on a modern Solaris 10 system via the Xhomer simulator
Core UNIX tools like vi, cc, and adb remain recognizable after two decades
The 1984 system lacks fundamental modern features: TCP/IP, virtual memory, multiprocessor support
The experience illustrates both UNIX's remarkable stability and its dramatic evolution
money_quotes: Using a twenty year old UNIX is a strange experience: I'm amazed at how little the most basic things have changed.
I was familiar with all of the tools necessary to write a program (vi), compile it (cc) and debug it (adb).

While it's amazing how familiar VENIX feels, I'm also stunned by how anemic its facilites are: it has no TCP/IP stack, no real filesystem, no multiple processor support, no resource management, no dynamic linking, no real virtual memory system, no observability and poor debuggability.
It reminds me how far we have come – even before we embarked on the far more radical technologies found in Solaris 10.
Seeing adb spit out a true Algol stack backtrace would be like sipping from the debugging fountain of youth.
posts.key_insight: UNIX's core abstractions have remained remarkably stable for decades, but the underlying systems infrastructure has evolved dramatically, reminding us that familiar interfaces can mask profound technological progress.

posts.summary: Cantrill shares his discovery of a DTrace blog written in Russian (or another Cyrillic language), expressing enthusiasm about the international reach of DTrace adoption. He humorously speculates about which specific Cyrillic language it might be, noting it's probably not Mansi or Yakut. He suggests the code examples are worth examining even without understanding the text and hints at getting a colleague to translate.
key_points: DTrace adoption is spreading internationally, with technical content appearing in Russian
Code examples in technical blogs can be valuable even without understanding the surrounding language
The open source/technical community crosses language barriers
money_quotes: Even if you only understand the examples, it's worth checking out.
posts.key_insight: Technical tools like DTrace transcend language barriers, with code examples serving as a universal language that makes international technical blogs valuable even to non-speakers.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill reminds readers about a DTrace challenge competition with only 17 days remaining. He encourages participation as a way to demonstrate DTrace skills and win prizes. As the creator of DTrace, he notes he's ineligible to compete but promises to share what his entry would have been after the contest concludes.
key_points: DTrace challenge competition has 17 days remaining
Competition offers prizes ('propellor-head bling bling')
Cantrill is ineligible as DTrace creator
He will share his hypothetical entry post-contest
money_quotes: This is a great opportunity to both show your DTrace prowess and win some propellor-head bling bling in the process.
posts.key_insight: A brief promotional post encouraging the DTrace community to participate in a skills competition, reflecting the early community-building efforts around the technology.

posts.summary: Cantrill defends and expands his earlier economic analysis of software, arguing that software has near-zero variable costs and doesn't wear out, which creates unique market dynamics. He uses the Firebird database story as a case study showing how proprietary vendors who get too greedy with pricing push customers past their 'FYO point' (threshold to switch), while open source alternatives benefit from both supply-side contributions and demand-side adoption. His core prediction is that open source will inevitably drive the switching cost toward zero for many software products, and companies must adapt by using open source to drive complementary goods or face obsolescence.
key_points: Software's variable cost of production is essentially zero - only bandwidth costs apply
Software doesn't wear out; bugs aren't degradation but pre-existing attributes being exposed
Open source creates powerful supply-side economics while demand-side adds features and lowers switching costs
Companies that overplay their hand on pricing push customers past their 'FYO point' to switch
The future belongs to companies that use open source to drive complementary goods
money_quotes: At the risk of stating a tautology, the cost of marketing a product is not a part of the variable cost of making the product; the variable cost is simply the cost of manufacturing one more unit, and (for software at least) that cost is damn near zero.

The software works exactly today as it did twenty years ago – it's as good as new. Some mistake software that stops working as having 'worn out', but this is an incorrect inference: the software itself has not changed; a heretofore unknown attribute has merely been exposed – an attribute that was there all along.

It may take years – and it may even take decades – but open source from the supply-side coupled with energy from the demand-side will ultimately drive the FYO point towards zero for many existing software products.

Companies that can use open source to drive complementary goods will survive and thrive; those that can't will slowly whither away, until one day their software is acquired for pennies-on-the-dollar by some company that just happens to have some complementary goods to sell.
posts.key_insight: Open source economics will inevitably commoditize proprietary software by driving switching costs toward zero, forcing companies to compete on complementary goods rather than software licensing.

posts.summary: Cantrill announces the first partial open-sourcing of Solaris through the release of DTrace source code, providing a detailed technical tour of the codebase layout and highlighting interesting implementation details. He walks through the directory structure (usr/src/cmd, usr/src/lib, usr/src/uts) and explains key libraries like libdtrace, libctf, and libproc. The post showcases several fascinating code snippets that demonstrate the complexity of systems programming, including circular list detection, compiler optimization workarounds, and SPARC register window manipulation.
key_points: DTrace source code released as the first component of OpenSolaris, marking an important milestone in operating systems history
The codebase follows traditional Unix layout with Bell Labs roots, organized into commands, libraries, and kernel code (uts = Unix Time-Sharing System)
DTrace skirts the halting problem by not allowing backwards branches in DIF, requiring special techniques like circular list detection for untrusted data structures
Implementation comments are deliberately placed in public header files following the Unix philosophy that system implementation should be described in publicly available headers
Source readability is a core value - questions about unclear code will result in both answers and improved comments
money_quotes: I view OpenSolaris as an important milestone in the history of operating systems – doubly so because it is Solaris 10 that we are open sourcing, an operating system that I believe to be an important historical milestone in its own right.

You may recognize this little trick as the answer to a stupid interview question -- one that always seems to be asked by those who had to have it laboriously explained to them, and who can't even concisely describe the conditions under which one would be forced to resort to this technique.
As you browse the DTrace source, you may well find yourself asking 'does it need to be this complicated?' The short answer is, in most cases, 'regrettably yes.'
The moral of the story is this: don't assume that someone asking you a question is an idiot – especially if the question is about the intricacies of SPARC DCTI couples.
We did this because we believe in the Unix idea that the system implementation should be described as much as possible in its publicly available header files.
posts.tone: enthusiastic and technically detailed, with characteristic Cantrill wit and self-deprecating humor
posts.key_insight: Opening source code is not just about transparency but about inviting others into the intellectual journey of understanding why complex systems software necessarily becomes complicated.

posts.summary: Cantrill announces the first advanced DTrace presentation, created in response to users who have outgrown the existing 400 pages of documentation. The presentation is a deliberately unstructured collection of tips, tricks, and gotchas that could be presented in any order. He acknowledges the presentation's dream-like quality due to its lack of narrative arc, but defends this approach as the best way to convey such arcane technical details.
key_points: Advanced DTrace users are reaching the limits of existing documentation
The presentation deliberately lacks narrative structure because the content is inherently random
Live demos are essential for grokking complex DTrace concepts
400 pages of documentation still isn't enough for power users
money_quotes: The time for an advanced talk was clearly overdue; many of the questions both in the DTrace forum and internally at Sun have indicated that the limits of the DTrace documentation are being reached.
Indeed, it's so random the slides could pretty much be in any order – there is no narrative arc to this presentation whatsoever.
Yes, there are limits to the documentation – which is pretty incredible considering that there's already 400 pages of it.
I've never had anyone fall asleep during one of my presentations.
posts.key_insight: Even comprehensive documentation has limits, and advanced users eventually need unstructured collections of hard-won practical knowledge that don't fit neatly into traditional educational formats.

posts.summary: Cantrill responds to concerns raised after a Chaos Computer Club presentation suggested DTrace could be a security risk. He explains that DTrace requires root privileges to use, meaning it can only be exploited on systems that have already been compromised—at which point attackers have far more dangerous options available. The presentation actually focused on designing kernel modules that evade DTrace detection, which reveals that DTrace is more threatening to attackers than useful to them.
key_points: DTrace requires root privileges and can only be used on already-compromised systems
Once a system is compromised, attackers have many more dangerous tools available than DTrace
The CCC presentation focused on evading DTrace, not exploiting it
DTrace's observability makes it a security asset, not a risk
money_quotes: The techniques described in the paper and in the presentation are only for use on a system that one has already compromised.
Of course, once a system is compromised, all bets are off.
Everything you can do with DTrace was in principle possible before DTrace – DTrace just happens to make it a little easier.

The fact that time and effort were spent on DTrace evasion is telling: as a tool designed to expose the inner workings of a production system, DTrace is much more feared by the Black Hats than it is useful to them.
Far from being a security risk, DTrace is very much a security asset.
posts.key_insight: Powerful observability tools like DTrace are more valuable for defenders detecting intrusions than for attackers who already have root access, as evidenced by malware authors' efforts to evade rather than exploit such tools.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that reverse engineering is a fundamental right for engineers, criticizing BitMover's decision to rescind the free BitKeeper license for Linux because Andrew Tridgell attempted to reverse engineer its protocols. He contends that understanding how systems work is essential to software development, and that companies should rely on patents—not obfuscation—to protect intellectual property. Cantrill defends Tridge against Linus Torvalds' criticism, framing the act of reverse engineering as a principled stand worth defending.
key_points: Reverse engineering is a natural right essential to software development
Patents, not obfuscation, should protect intellectual property
BitMover's license revocation was cowardly and exhibited 'Bad ISV Behavior'
DTrace was created partly to enable reverse engineering
Tridge's actions represent standing up for principle, not wrongdoing
money_quotes: I (like many engineers, I suspect) view reverse engineering as a Natural Right. That is, I believe that we are endowed with certain unalienable Rights, and that among these are Life, Liberty and the pursuit of Understanding how the hell something works.
It should be the protection afforded by patents – and not the obfuscation inherent in a running system – that prevents the rip-off artists.

In my experience, when developing a layer in the stack of software abstraction, you always need to understand at least one layer below you and you often need to understand at least one layer above you – and reverse engineering is often the primary means to achieve this understanding.
To rescind the free BK license simply because someone dared to even understand how it works is just…cowardly.
Tridge 'ruined it for everyone' just like Rosa Parks and Helen Gahagan Douglas and Nathan Hale and anyone else who ever took a stand for what was right.
posts.key_insight: Reverse engineering is not just legally defensible but a fundamental engineering right, and companies that try to prevent it through licensing rather than patents are exhibiting cowardice and hostility toward their own users.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill demonstrates the exciting new capability of using DTrace to dynamically trace Java applications through JVMTI/JVMPI agents. Using a Java Tetris game as an example, he walks through various DTrace techniques to monitor method calls, aggregate method invocations, capture Java stack traces, and track object allocations in real-time. While noting the limitation that the JVM must be restarted with the agent (making it more suitable for development than production), he emphasizes this represents unprecedented visibility into the full software stack from Java through native code to the kernel.
key_points: JVMTI/JVMPI agents now allow DTrace to instrument Java applications with probes for method entry/exit, garbage collection, thread events, and more
The ability to trace code flow from Java through native code into the kernel and back is 'without precedent'
DTrace's aggregation capabilities make it easy to identify hot methods and understand call patterns
The jstack() action enables capturing Java stack traces to understand why specific methods are being called
Real-time monitoring of object allocation rates can help identify pathological behavior in Java applications
money_quotes: Such a view of code flow is without precedent – it is awesome (in all senses of that word) to see the progression through the stack of software abstraction.
damn is it a big leap forward
This is way too much output to sift through, so let's instead aggregate on class and method name
That doesn't seem so bad, but you can easily see how useful this is going to be on those pathological Java apps!
posts.key_insight: DTrace's integration with Java through JVMTI agents provides unprecedented observability into the entire software stack, enabling developers to trace execution flow from high-level Java code through native libraries down to kernel calls.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill highlights Sean McGrath's work on adding DTrace probes to Python, acknowledging it as an impressive early effort despite its limitations. He teases upcoming infrastructure improvements that will enable user-level providers to match the semantic richness of kernel-level providers. The post reflects the early excitement around extending DTrace's observability capabilities to dynamic languages.
key_points: Sean McGrath created experimental DTrace probes for Python
Current solution has limitations but demonstrates possibilities
New infrastructure in development will enable richer user-level providers
User-level providers will eventually match kernel-level semantic richness
money_quotes: As Sean points out, this isn't a perfect solution – but damn is it cool!
Once completed, this infrastructure will allow for user-level providers to export the same semantic richness as the stable kernel-level providers like io, sched and proc.
But as Sean's work shows, you can still do very interesting things even with what exists today…
posts.key_insight: Even imperfect early implementations of DTrace probes in dynamic languages like Python demonstrate the powerful potential for user-level observability that would eventually be fully realized.

posts.summary: Cantrill celebrates the release of OpenSolaris by offering a deep technical tour through one of the kernel's most intricate subsystems: the turnstile mechanism for priority inheritance. He traces the discovery and resolution of a subtle design defect in user-level priority inheritance that caused false deadlock panics, detailing how he and Jeff Bonwick collaborated intensely to understand and fix the problem. The post serves both as technical documentation and as a demonstration of the kind of deep systems thinking required to work on operating system internals.
key_points: Priority inheritance for user-level locks requires kernel-level state tracking, which creates complex lock ordering challenges
A design defect in Solaris 8's user-level priority inheritance caused false deadlock detection and system panics
The fix required distinguishing between real application deadlocks and transient false deadlocks caused by the kernel's internal locking
Intense collaborative debugging ('the mind-meld') was essential to solving a problem where every proposed solution introduced new issues
money_quotes: Solaris is the New York City of operating systems: it is what you don't see that makes what you do see possible – and like New York City, it's what you don't see that's really interesting.

This is one of the most gratifying feelings in software engineering: analyzing a failure postmortem, discovering that the bug should be easily reproduced, writing a test case testing the hypothesis, and then watching the system blow up just as you predicted. Nothing quite compares to this feeling; it's the software equivalent of the walk-off home run.

After exhausting ourselves on the alternatives, we were forced to the conclusion that the in-kernel lock was a constraint on the problem – and our focus switched from avoiding the situation to detecting it.

I won't go into details about why this is the case – it's left as an exercise to the reader – but suffice it to say it's one of the many reasons that we have joked about requiring a license to grab thread lock.
If you find it (and you can explain it fully), and you're in the job market, we'll fly you out for an interview.
posts.key_insight: The most critical parts of operating systems are often invisible infrastructure requiring extraordinary care and deep collaboration to get right, and opening source code invites others into that hidden world of complexity.

posts.summary: Cantrill celebrates the OpenSolaris launch day by curating the overwhelming flood of technical blog posts from Sun engineers. He highlights key entries across fault management, virtual memory, linking/loading, and scheduling subsystems, providing guided tours through the intricacies of Solaris kernel internals. The post serves as both a celebration of the grass-roots open source effort and a reading guide to help the community navigate over 200,000 words of technical content published in a single day.
key_points: OpenSolaris launched with hundreds of blog entries totaling over 200,000 words (800+ pages) in one day
The post curates technical deep-dives across fault management, virtual memory, linking/loading, and scheduling
Engineers finally get to publicly discuss the system they've poured their lives into
The breadth of blogging demonstrates this is a genuine grass-roots effort, not corporate mandate
money_quotes: The breadth and depth of our blogging will hopefully put to rest any notion that open sourcing Solaris isn't a grass-roots effort: if nothing else, it should be clear that we in the trenches are very excited to finally be able to talk about the system that we have poured so much of our lives into – and to welcome new would-be contributors into the fold.
One of the most polished subsystems in Solaris is the linker and loader – the craftsmanship of the engineers that have built it has been an ongoing inspiration for many of us in Solaris development.
If nothing else, you'll see from David's work the research element of operating system development: we often aren't assured of success when we endeavor to improve the system.
You'll see from Eric's odyssey that scheduling problems can require a lot of brain-bending (and patience) to debug!
posts.key_insight: The massive, spontaneous outpouring of deeply technical blog posts from Solaris engineers on OpenSolaris launch day demonstrates that open sourcing was a genuine grass-roots effort driven by engineers eager to share the systems they'd spent their careers building.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces he will be giving a talk and demo on DTrace at the BayLISA meeting in Cupertino. He emphasizes that he'll be demonstrating DTrace running on OpenSolaris bits that he downloaded and built himself from outside Sun's internal network, highlighting the newly open nature of the project.
key_points: DTrace demo at BayLISA meeting in Cupertino
Running OpenSolaris built from publicly available source code
Demonstrates accessibility of OpenSolaris to external developers
money_quotes: I will be demo'ing DTrace tonight on my laptop running OpenSolaris bits that I downloaded and built myself from outside of Sun's internal network.
posts.key_insight: The ability to build and run OpenSolaris entirely from publicly available sources outside Sun's network marked a significant milestone in the project's openness.

posts.summary: Cantrill continues cataloging the flood of blog posts from OpenSolaris's Opening Day launch, organizing them into categories including system calls, build process and workspace management, and debuggability. He highlights how Sun engineers are using blogs to document previously unwritten tribal knowledge about Solaris internals. The post emphasizes the Solaris team's obsession with debuggability and reliability, noting they built custom tools like mdb and DTrace because existing tools were insufficient for their needs.
key_points: Blogs serve as the most scalable way to transfer knowledge from those who have it to those who need it
The Solaris team requires lint-clean code and takes build process integrity extremely seriously
Debuggability is central to Solaris development ethos - the OS must be reliable above all else
The team built custom debugging tools (mdb, DTrace) because existing tools couldn't meet their needs
Early workspace management tools were 'all blade and no handle' - useful but dangerous
money_quotes: Blogs have become a critical conduit of information for us – we believe that they are the most scalable way to get information from the people who have it to the people who need it.
This is an accurate reflection of our ethos in developing Solaris: the operating system must be reliable above all else, and we view debugging the operating system as our primary responsibility.
This responsibility runs deeper than just the act of debugging, because our needs so outstripped existing tools that we designed and built our own.

For a long time, wx was a shell script in Bonwick's home directory. It was incredibly useful, but it was also easy to accidentally blow your brains out. As Bart is fond of saying, it was 'all blade and no handle.'
We require the Solaris source to be lint-clean.
posts.key_insight: Building reliable systems software requires not just debugging skills but also building custom debugging tools and designing subsystems with debuggability as a first-class concern.

posts.summary: Cantrill catalogs numerous 'debugging war stories' from OpenSolaris engineers' blog posts, using them to illustrate a key cultural value: in Solaris development, the highest peer respect comes not from building flashy new features or achieving performance wins, but from hunting down and fixing diabolical bugs that have plagued the system for years. He highlights the craftsmanship ethos of doing things the 'Right Way' - understanding code deeply, developing proper fixes, and writing test cases even for small issues.
key_points: In Solaris culture, debugging difficult bugs earns more peer respect than building new features or performance wins
Bug-hunting is recommended as the path for newcomers to make a name for themselves in OpenSolaris
Engineers obsess over bugs to the point of memorizing bug ID numbers
The satisfaction comes from the full cycle: understanding strange code, developing the right fix, verifying it, and writing test cases
Kernel memory corruption is described as 'surely the most pernicious of software pathologies'
money_quotes: In some organizations, you're a hero among engineers if you come up with a whizzy new app (even if the implementation is a cesspool), while in others you might gain respect among engineers by getting a big performance win (even if it makes things a bit flakey), but in Solaris development, nothing gains the respect of peers like debugging some diabolical bug that has plagued the operating system for years.
Every once in a while, a bug sticks with you long enough that you remember the ID number without having to think about it.
The dedication to this kind of craftsmanship – taking the time to do it the Right Way, even for small stuff – is what has drawn many of us to Solaris.
It's a good example of how something that appears simple can be stubbornly complicated.
posts.key_insight: Engineering culture is defined by what earns peer respect, and in systems software, that respect flows to those who hunt down and properly fix the hardest bugs rather than those who ship flashy but flawed new features.

posts.summary: This post is a curated roundup of technical blog entries written by Sun Microsystems engineers for the OpenSolaris launch, covering topics from Zones containerization to the Solaris Volume Manager to various kernel internals. Cantrill highlights the depth and breadth of engineering content produced on Opening Day, noting his pleasant surprise at how many engineers contributed detailed technical explanations. The post serves as both a directory of valuable technical resources and a celebration of the engineering culture that made OpenSolaris possible.
key_points: Engineers contributed far more technical content for OpenSolaris launch than expected
Solaris Volume Manager is an underappreciated bundled feature that could save licensing fees vs third-party solutions
User-level service development can be more challenging than kernel development, contrary to some assumptions
The OpenSolaris launch represented a significant moment of transparency and community building
money_quotes: When we were planning the launch and talking about the possibility of dealing with a massive amount of Opening Day content, my response was 'hurt me with that problem.'
Despite the fact that it could save them gobs in licensing fees to certain third parties, many seem to not realize that we bundle an industrial-strength volume manager with Solaris.
Peter's work is a good example of why – contrary to the beliefs of some pinheads – developing a user-level service provider can be quite a bit more challenging than developing in the kernel.
Well, as it turns out, most engineers didn't need much convincing – many provided rich, deep content – and I was indeed hurt with that problem!
posts.key_insight: The overwhelming volume of deep technical content from engineers on OpenSolaris launch day demonstrated that developers are eager to share their work when given the platform and permission to do so.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill reports from JavaOne 2005 where DTrace was featured in the morning keynote, with Java support announced to audience applause. Adam Leventhal gave a live demo using real DTrace scripts rather than pre-canned presentation software, and the scripts were published so anyone could replicate the demo. The post also promotes an OpenSolaris BOF session and highlights Solaris's growing appeal to Java developers following IBM's announcement of Solaris support.
key_points: DTrace Java support announced at JavaOne keynote to enthusiastic response
Live demo used actual DTrace rather than canned demo software
Demo scripts were open-sourced and blogged for public use
IBM announced Solaris support the same day
OpenSolaris BOF session held to showcase Solaris technologies to Java developers
money_quotes: The most remarkable aspect of which is that it was actually DTrace and not the canned, gussied-up gimcrack that normally passes for a keynote demo.
In fact, this demo was so tangibly real, Adam has blogged the scripts from the demo. With these scripts, you can now give Adam's keynote in the comfort of your own home!
Solaris has never been more attractive to the Java developer.
posts.key_insight: Authentic technical demos that share their actual code are more valuable and credible than polished but fake keynote presentations.

posts.summary: Cantrill demonstrates how DTrace can be used to diagnose GNOME desktop startup performance issues by tracing I/O operations and CPU sampling during login. His analysis reveals that GConf's XML backend accounts for over a quarter of all I/O, shared object loading causes a 'death of a thousand cuts,' and symlink resolution alone may cost a full second of startup time. The post serves as both a practical debugging tutorial and an advertisement for DTrace's capabilities in understanding complex system behavior.
key_points: GConf's XML backend causes over 25% of all I/O during GNOME startup
Shared object text and read-only data loading accounts for nearly a third of I/O
Symlink chasing during startup (nearly 80 symlinks) may add a full second of blocked I/O time
CPU utilization is dwarfed by I/O costs as the main performance bottleneck
Icon file loading is another significant I/O contributor, addressed in GTK+ 2.6
money_quotes: This is classic death-of-a-thousand-cuts, and it's hard to see that there's an easy way to fix this.
Over a quarter of all I/O is from GConf – and many of these are from wandering around an expansive directory hierarchy looking for configuration information.
CPU utilization doesn't look like a big issue – or it's one that is at least dwarfed by the cost of performing I/O.
We might be spending as much as one second of GNOME startup blocked on I/O just to chase symlinks! Ouch!

Having an app link to libpango-1.0.so.0 and not libpango-1.0.so.400.1 is clearly a Good Thing, and having this be a symlink instead of a hardlink is clearly a Good Thing – but all of that goodness leaves you with a read dependency that's hard to work around.
posts.key_insight: DTrace's ability to trace I/O and CPU activity system-wide reveals that GNOME startup slowness stems primarily from scattered filesystem reads rather than CPU usage, with GConf and symlink resolution being major culprits.

posts.summary: DTrace's most fundamental principle is safety: it must never accidentally cause system failure, which is what enables its use on production systems. The post explains three key architectural decisions that ensure this safety: instrumentation providers that only expose safe probe points, a custom virtual machine (DIF) instead of native code execution, and careful exception handling for runtime errors. These deliberate design choices trade some flexibility for absolute safety guarantees.
key_points: DTrace providers guarantee instrumentation safety by only exposing safe probe points
Probe context must never call into any kernel facilities to prevent recursion
Native code execution in the kernel is unsafe due to stores, loops, and illegal instructions
The DIF virtual machine has no backwards branches and no arbitrary stores by design
Runtime errors like division-by-zero and unmapped memory loads are handled gracefully
money_quotes: DTrace must not be able to accidentally induce system failure. It is our strict adherence to this principle that allows DTrace to be used with confidence on production systems – and it is its use on production systems that most fundamentally separates DTrace from what has come before it.
While in probe context, DTrace itself must not call into any facilities in the kernel at-large.
The alternative – to execute user-specified code natively in the kernel – is untenable from a safety perspective.
While heuristics are fine when trying to achieve performance, they are not acceptable when correctness is the constraint.
This may seem unnecessarily severe (this makes loops an impossibility by architecture), but to us it was an acceptable tradeoff to achieve absolute safety.
DTrace is not safe by accident – DTrace is safe by deliberate design and by careful execution.
posts.key_insight: True safety in systems software requires making unsafe operations architecturally impossible rather than relying on detection or heuristics.

posts.summary: Cantrill highlights a blog post by Brian Utterback that demonstrates DTrace's power in debugging a notoriously difficult NTP problem involving mysterious signals killing an application. He uses this as a teaching moment to emphasize that while DTrace makes previously unsolvable problems tractable, it remains a tool that requires human insight and expertise to wield effectively. The complexity inherent in misbehaving systems doesn't disappear—DTrace just makes it possible to understand that complexity.
key_points: DTrace enabled debugging of a 'canonically nasty' problem that was previously nearly impossible to diagnose
Tools amplify human capability but don't replace the need for expertise and insight
Systems complexity is endemic and irreducible—better tools just make it more accessible
money_quotes: The pathology – a signal of mysterious origin killing an app – is a canonically nasty problem and (before DTrace) it was very difficult (or damned near impossible?) to diagnose.
DTrace is a tool, not a magician. That is, DTrace still needs to be used by someone with a brain.

Systems – especially misbehaving or suboptimal ones – have endemic complexity. We have tried to make it as easy as possible to use DTrace to understand that complexity, but the complexity exists nonetheless.
DTrace allows you to solve problems that were previously unsolvable (or damned near) – but it means you'll be using your brain more, not less, so you'd better stop stabbing it with Q-tips.
posts.key_insight: Powerful debugging tools don't eliminate the need for human expertise—they expand the frontier of problems that skilled engineers can actually solve.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces his two DTrace presentations at OSCON 2005. The first is a free hands-on tutorial covering OpenSolaris development with DTrace, while the second is a shorter session focusing on DTrace fundamentals and its relationship with open source projects. He invites attendees to connect with him during the conference.
key_points: Free tutorial on OpenSolaris development with DTrace on Tuesday
Demo-intensive introduction to DTrace and related tooling
Thursday session focuses on DTrace and open source intersection
Open invitation for conference attendees to connect
money_quotes: We're going to keep it very hands-on, and it will be a demo-intensive introduction to both DTrace and to our larger tool-set that we use to both build OpenSolaris and to diagnose it when it all goes horribly wrong.

This presentation is going to give a quick review of the DTrace fundamentals, and then focus on the confluence of DTrace and open source – both what DTrace can do for your open source project, and what you can do (if you're so inclined) for the DTrace open source project.
posts.key_insight: DTrace represents a bidirectional opportunity for open source: it can help developers understand their projects while also benefiting from community contributions.

posts.summary: A brief invitation to an OpenSolaris after-party at OSCON 2005. Cantrill promotes the event with promises of premium drinks and humorous predictions about a colleague's frustration with gcc's SPARC backend potentially leading to furniture being thrown out windows.
key_points: OpenSolaris hosted social events at major conferences to build community
The gcc SPARC backend was notoriously problematic, likened to the Ford Pinto
money_quotes: If for nothing else, you'll want to be there in the early morning hours when Keith throws the furniture out the window in a drunken rage against gcc's SPARC backend.
