posts.key_insight: Community building through informal social events was an important part of OpenSolaris's strategy to attract developers and build goodwill at open source conferences.

posts.summary: At OSCON 2005, PHP core developer Wez Furlong created a DTrace provider for PHP during the OpenSolaris BOF session. The provider exposes function-entry and function-return probes that allow developers to trace PHP function calls, aggregate by file and line number, and measure function execution time. Crucially, all of this can be done on production systems across any number of PHP/Apache processes without modification or restart.
key_points: Wez Furlong implemented DTrace support for PHP as a loadable shared object extension
The provider exposes function-entry and function-return probes with function name, file name, and line number arguments
DTrace enables production-safe tracing across hundreds of Apache/PHP processes simultaneously
Users can aggregate function calls, quantize by line number, and measure execution time with simple one-liners
money_quotes: After a little bit of work (and a little bit of debugging), we got it working – and damn is it cool.
And because it's DTrace, this can all be done on a production box – and without regard to the number of PHP processes.
So if you have 200 Apache processes handling PHP, the above invocations would aggregate across them.
It's the kind of thing that needs to be seen to be appreciated.
posts.key_insight: DTrace's ability to dynamically instrument PHP in production without modification or performance penalty represents a fundamental shift in how developers can understand and debug their applications at scale.

posts.summary: Cantrill provides a detailed walkthrough of using DTrace to instrument PHP applications, building from simple function tracing to full-stack observability including user-level code, libc calls, and kernel functions. The post demonstrates how DTrace's PHP provider (developed by Wez Furlong) enables developers to trace function entries and returns with file and line information. Through progressively more complex examples, he shows how to aggregate across multiple PHP instances and trace the complete execution path from PHP through native code into the kernel.
key_points: DTrace PHP provider adds function-entry and function-return probes with function name, file, and line number arguments
DTrace can aggregate data across multiple PHP instances to understand system-wide PHP behavior
Thread-local variables enable following code flow through specific execution paths
Full-stack tracing reveals how PHP functions induce work in libc and the kernel
All instrumentation can be done safely in production systems
money_quotes: DTrace allows you to aggregate across PHP instances, allowing you to understand not just what a particular PHP is doing, but what PHP is doing more generally.
This shows us what's really going on – or at least more of what's really going on: our PHP functions are inducing work from libc.
Now we're seeing everything that our PHP program is doing, from the PHP through the native code, into the kernel and back.
Using DTrace on PHP has a number of unique advantages: you can look at the entire system, and you can look at the entire stack – and you can do it all in production.
posts.key_insight: DTrace's ability to trace from high-level PHP code through native libraries into the kernel provides unprecedented full-stack visibility that traditional debugging tools cannot match, all while being safe for production use.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill recounts a lunch meeting with Ubuntu's Jeff Waugh during LinuxWorld, where they discussed the potential of combining the best features of both Linux and Solaris ecosystems. The group envisioned an ideal system that would merge Debian/Ubuntu package management with Solaris Service Management Facility and DTrace. Cantrill muses whether OpenSolaris could make this cross-pollination possible, while playfully questioning whether licensing 'original sin' (the CDDL vs GPL incompatibility) would prevent such a union.
key_points: Cross-community collaboration between Ubuntu and Solaris developers
Vision of combining best-of-breed technologies from both ecosystems
OpenSolaris as potential bridge between Linux and Solaris worlds
Licensing incompatibility as the barrier to integration
money_quotes: I think everyone agreed that a system with Debian packages, Ubuntu package management, the Solaris Service Management Facility and (of course) DTrace would be one hell of a system.
And with OpenSolaris, this paradise seems tantalizingly attainable – or does original sin prevent us from reentering the garden?
posts.key_insight: The best operating system would cherry-pick excellent components from different projects, but licensing incompatibilities between open source communities often prevent this ideal integration.

posts.summary: Cantrill announces that Devon O'Dell has begun serious work on porting DTrace to FreeBSD, enabled by OpenSolaris's CDDL license which permits component ports to other systems. While others had previously talked about such a port, O'Dell is the first to actually begin the work. Cantrill acknowledges the complexity of the task given DTrace's Solaris-specific dependencies but expresses confidence it can be done with community collaboration.
key_points: The CDDL license enables porting OpenSolaris components like DTrace to other operating systems
Devon O'Dell is the first person to seriously begin work on a FreeBSD port of DTrace
DTrace has complex dependencies on Solaris-specific components that make porting non-trivial
Team DTrace is offering support and collaboration to anyone working on the port
money_quotes: One of the exciting things about OpenSolaris is that it's released under a license – the CDDL – that allows ports of individual components to other systems.
This has been talked about before (in some cases, with braggadocio), but Devon is the first to start the work in earnest.
And indeed, work it will be: DTrace isn't a simple system, and it has several dependencies on other, Solaris-specific system components.
posts.key_insight: Open source licensing like the CDDL enables cross-pollination of sophisticated systems software between operating systems, but actually doing the work matters more than talking about it.

posts.summary: Cantrill announces a prototype DTrace provider for Ruby developed by Rich Lowe, demonstrating its power through increasingly sophisticated D scripts that trace method calls, object initialization, and call hierarchies. Using Ruby's calendar script as an example, he shows how DTrace can reveal performance issues like the surprisingly complex implementation of Date#+() which induces dozens of method calls due to rational number usage. The post argues that while Ruby has existing profiling tools via set_trace_func, DTrace offers fundamentally superior capabilities for understanding application behavior.
key_points: Rich Lowe has created a prototype Ruby DTrace provider with function-entry and function-return probes
DTrace can trace Ruby method calls, showing class, method, file, and line number information
The calendar example reveals Date#+() is surprisingly expensive, inducing 52+ method calls per invocation
DTrace advantages over Ruby's set_trace_func include aggregations, thread-local variables, and cross-stack visibility
Running Ruby scripts can be instrumented without stopping or restarting them
money_quotes: That's a lot of work for something that should be pretty simple! Indeed, it's counterintuitive that, say, Integer#gcd() would be called from Date#+() – and it certainly seems suboptimal.

The Ruby DTrace provider is nonetheless a great lurch forward for Ruby developers: it allows developers to use DTrace-specific constructs like aggregations and thread-local variables to hone in on a problem.
It allows Ruby-related work performed lower in the stack (e.g., in the I/O subsystem, CPU dispatcher or network stack) to be connected to the Ruby code inducing it.

It allows a running Ruby script to be instrumented (and reinstrumented) without stopping or restarting it; and it allows multiple, disjoint Ruby scripts to be coherently observed and understood as a single entity.
More succinctly, it's just damned cool.
posts.key_insight: DTrace's ability to dynamically instrument running applications and connect high-level language behavior to low-level system activity represents a quantum leap beyond traditional profiling tools.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces his recognition as one of MIT Technology Review's TR35, the top 35 innovators under 35. He expresses genuine humility about the honor, noting that other recipients are working on seemingly more significant problems like cancer cures and rocket science. The post is characteristically self-deprecating while acknowledging the achievement.
key_points: Named to MIT Technology Review's TR35 list of top innovators under 35
Expresses humility by comparing his work to cancer research and rocket science
References a podcast interview about the award
money_quotes: It's a great honor, especially because the other honorees are actually working on things like cures for cancer and rocket science – domains that I have known only as rhetorical flourish.
Should you like to hear me make a jackass out of myself on the subject, you might want to check out Richard Giles's latest I/O podcast.
posts.key_insight: Even significant industry recognition feels humbling when viewed alongside work in fields like medicine and aerospace that have more immediate life-or-death implications.

posts.summary: Cantrill recounts his first meeting with Andy Bechtolsheim after Sun acquired Kealia, describing an electric encounter between two engineers passionate about their respective crafts. Andy immediately launched into whiteboard diagrams of his hardware designs without introduction, while Cantrill fired back with Solaris 10 features at unprecedented speed. The meeting revealed Andy's rare ability to understand both hardware and software systems holistically, leading to mutual excitement about combining their innovations. This experience explains Cantrill's bullishness on Sun and celebrates the launch of Andy's new machines.
key_points: Andy Bechtolsheim exceeded even his legendary reputation as an engineer
The meeting was characterized by intense, rapid technical exchange
Andy demonstrated unusual cross-domain expertise spanning hardware and software
Their complementary innovations in hardware and software promised unique systems
money_quotes: Here, at long last, was the Real Deal: a fabled engineer who didn't disappoint – a giant who dwarfed the substantial legend that proceeded him.
This might have been one of the few times in my life where I thought I actually needed to speak faster to stay in front of my audience.

Most impressive of all, Andy had a keen intuition for the system – he immediately saw how his innovative hardware and our innovative software could combine to deliver some uniquely innovative systems to our customers.

Not everyone can own a car designed by Enzo Ferrari or a lamp crafted by Louis Comfort Tiffany – but at just over two grand a pop, pretty much everyone can own a machine designed by the greatest single-board computer designer in history.
posts.key_insight: True engineering greatness manifests not just in deep domain expertise but in the ability to see how different pieces of a system can combine to create something greater than the sum of its parts.

posts.summary: Cantrill expresses excitement about the Nexenta project, which combines Debian package management with the OpenSolaris kernel - something he had wished for but assumed was years away. Just three months after speculating about the possibilities of merging these systems, the Nexenta team released working ISOs that he immediately installed on his laptop. He demonstrates the combination's power by using DTrace to examine the Debian package management system.
key_points: The Nexenta project combines Debian package management (APT) with the OpenSolaris kernel
Cantrill had assumed such a combination would take years to develop, but it arrived in months
He successfully installed and ran Nexenta on his Acer Ferrari 3400 laptop
The combination allows using DTrace to examine Linux userland tools like package management
money_quotes: To be honest, when I wrote that I assumed that such a Xanadu would be a couple of years off at least – combining these systems is non-trivial.
Of course, hearing about a new technology is one thing; seeing it is quite another – I wanted to get my hands on actual bits before I got too excited.
Oh be still, my beating heart!
posts.key_insight: The open source community can achieve ambitious technical integrations far faster than even optimistic insiders expect when motivated developers tackle the problem directly.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces the public availability of ZFS, calling it the most important revolution in storage software in two decades. He traces the filesystem's origins to Jeff Bonwick's ambitious vision five years earlier and highlights ZFS's three pillars: dramatically simplified administration, unprecedented reliability through detection and correction of hardware errors, and excellent performance achieved through smarter software with more context. The post serves as a comprehensive tour of blog entries from the ZFS development team explaining various features and the grueling effort behind the release.
key_points: ZFS was designed from the ground up around unreliable hardware, detecting and correcting errors that other filesystems silently propagate
GUI admin tools should make something already simple even simpler, not smear lipstick over hideously over-complicated systems
Traditional storage reliability came from expensive hardware compensating for dumb software; ZFS inverts this with smart software enabling cheap hardware
ZFS delivers great performance because it has more context, a consequence of the end-to-end principle
Features like quotas, reservations, and ACLs were designed into ZFS from the start, not carpet-bombed over the source after the fact
money_quotes: I remember thinking 'he's either missed something basic that somehow invalidates these ideas – or this is the most important development in storage since RAID.'
ZFS is the most important revolution in storage software in two decades – and may be the most important idea since the filesystem itself.
GUI admin tools should make something that's already simple even simpler. They should not be used to smear lipstick over a hideously over-complicated system.
We've compensated for dumb software by having smart (and expensive) hardware. ZFS flips the economics on its head: smart software allows for stupid (and cheap) hardware – with ultra-high reliability.
How much of your data is being corrupted today? Yeah – scary, ain't it?
Like quotas and reservations, ACLs were a part of the design of ZFS – not something that was carpet-bombed over the source after the fact.
posts.key_insight: Revolutionary systems software comes from starting with a blank sheet of paper, designing around real-world constraints like unreliable hardware, and inverting traditional economics by making software smarter rather than demanding expensive hardware.

posts.summary: Cantrill highlights two approaches to getting DTrace on Linux: porting DTrace directly, or running Linux binaries under Solaris using the BrandZ framework. He points to Adam Leventhal's work demonstrating the latter approach, where DTrace was used to diagnose a serious Linux-specific performance problem in the 'top' utility. The post showcases how Solaris's compatibility layer enables powerful debugging tools to be applied to Linux applications.
key_points: DTrace can be brought to other operating systems either through porting or by running those systems under Solaris
BrandZ framework enables Linux binaries to run under Solaris
DTrace was used to find a serious performance problem in the Linux 'top' utility
money_quotes: There are two ways to get DTrace for another operating system: you can try porting DTrace to the other system, or you can use the new BrandZ framework to get that other system running under Solaris.
Pretty eff'in cool…
posts.key_insight: Rather than waiting for tools to be ported to your platform, sometimes it's more practical to run your platform under a system that already has those tools.

posts.summary: Cantrill announces that DTrace can now be used with Ruby on Rails in production thanks to 'is-enabled probes' that achieve near-zero disabled probe effect (0.2% vs the previous 2X overhead). He demonstrates this with DTrace scripts that profile Rails applications, showing system calls, Ruby method invocations, and MySQL queries per request. The post positions this as an important capability for Solaris as Rails becomes a significant growth area.
key_points: Is-enabled probes allow DTrace Ruby provider to have essentially zero disabled probe effect (0.2%)
Zero disabled probe effect is critical for production deployment of observability tools
DTrace can now trace the full stack: system calls, Ruby methods, and database queries in Rails apps
Ruby on Rails is identified as a hugely important growth area for Solaris
money_quotes: This provider represents a quantum leap for Ruby observability, but it suffers from the fact that we must do work (in particular, we must get the class and method) even when disabled.

This is undesirable (especially considering that the effect can be quite significant – up to 2X), and it runs against the DTrace ethos of zero disabled probe effect, but there has been no better solution.
Having zero disabled probe effect allows us to deploy DTrace on Ruby in production – which in turn opens up a whole new domain for DTrace: Ruby on Rails.
While this gives us lots of questions we might want to answer (e.g., 'why the hell are we doing 340 stats on every 'effing request!')
posts.key_insight: Production-safe observability requires zero disabled probe effect—you can't instrument what you can't afford to leave running.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill celebrates John Birrell's progress porting DTrace to FreeBSD, noting that while the port isn't complete, it's already useful for production systems and essential for kernel development. He frames the port as a triple win: FreeBSD users gain powerful observability tools, DTrace users get another platform and larger community, and OpenSolaris is validated as a genuinely open platform contributing to the open source ecosystem.
key_points: John Birrell has core DTrace functionality working on FreeBSD after months of work
The port is already useful for production FreeBSD systems and essential for kernel developers
DTrace on FreeBSD validates OpenSolaris as a truly open platform
Cross-platform ports benefit all communities involved through shared experiences
money_quotes: Once you've done kernel development with DTrace, there's no going back.
If you run FreeBSD in production, you're going to want John's port as it stands today – and if you develop for the FreeBSD kernel (drivers or otherwise), you're going to need it.

The presence of a FreeBSD port of DTrace validates that OpenSolaris is an open, innovative platform (despite what some buttheads say) – one that will benefit from and contribute to the undeniable economics of open source.
posts.key_insight: Cross-platform open source ports create virtuous cycles that benefit all participating communities while validating the openness of the originating project.

posts.summary: Apple announced DTrace support for Mac OS X Leopard at WWDC, representing a significant engineering achievement that includes implementing the FBT provider for PowerPC. Cantrill celebrates this as validation of the OpenSolaris community's open source approach, directly countering IBM's criticism that they weren't operating in the 'spirit' of open source. The port, while not yet complete, is functional enough to be useful and will bring DTrace to a much broader audience of developers.
key_points: Apple ported DTrace to Mac OS X Leopard with substantial engineering work
The port includes FBT provider implementation for PowerPC architecture
Apple is using DTrace as foundation for their new Xray performance tool
The collaboration validates OpenSolaris community's open source approach
money_quotes: Often (or even usually?) announcements at conferences are more vapor than ware. In this case, though, Apple is being quite modest: they have done a tremendous amount of engineering work to bring DTrace to their platform.

The entire OpenSolaris community should feel proud about this development because it gives the lie to IBM's nauseating assertion that we in the OpenSolaris community aren't in the 'spirit' of open source.
It will be a relief to finally have DTrace on the only computer under my roof that doesn't run Solaris!
While it's not yet a complete port, it's certainly enough to be uniquely useful – and it was quite a thrill to see Objective C frames in a ustack action!
posts.key_insight: Cross-platform adoption of open source technology like DTrace validates the collaborative spirit of open source communities, regardless of corporate criticism about licensing approaches.

posts.summary: Cantrill responds to IBM's criticism that OpenSolaris is a 'facade' by dismissing it as a corporate temper tantrum and offering constructive alternatives. He proposes several ways IBM could contribute to open source, from the legally difficult (open-sourcing OS/2 or AIX) to the culturally challenging (supporting OpenSolaris ports to IBM hardware). His most serious suggestion is for IBM to port DTrace to AIX, which would benefit IBM customers and allow IBM to participate meaningfully in the open source community without giving up any IP.
key_points: IBM accused OpenSolaris of being a 'facade', which Cantrill dismisses as corporate posturing
Legal encumbrances from the 'Bad Old Days' of Unix licensing make open-sourcing AIX or OS/2 impractical
IBM could support OpenSolaris ports to POWER or S/390 without assisting Sun hardware sales
Porting DTrace to AIX would directly benefit IBM customers and is technically feasible as Apple demonstrated
The post frames IBM's criticism as counterproductive compared to constructive open source collaboration
money_quotes: I would liken IBM's latest outburst to something of a temper tantrum – and as with a screaming toddler, the best way to deal with this is to not reward the performance, but rather to offer some constructive alternatives.
AIX is one of the true enterprise-class operating systems – one with a long history of running business-critical applications.
Sun doesn't sell POWER-based gear, so you would have the comfort of knowing that your efforts would in no way assist a Sun hardware sale.
Your customers want it. Apple has shown that it can be done. We'll help you do it.
So to IBM I say what parents have said to screaming toddlers for time immemorial: we're ready when you are.
posts.key_insight: Rather than engaging in corporate mudslinging about open source credentials, companies like IBM would better serve their customers by actually contributing to open source projects like DTrace that deliver tangible technical benefits.

posts.summary: Cantrill follows up on a months-delayed promise to release DTrace diffs for Ruby on Rails with is-enabled probes. He uses the occasion to enthusiastically promote Brendan Gregg's Helper Monkey tool, which brings DTrace-style debugging capabilities to JavaScript. The post highlights the desperate state of JavaScript debugging at the time and positions Helper Monkey as a transformative advancement for AJAX development.
key_points: Released Ruby 1.8.2 DTrace diffs with is-enabled probes after months of delay
Helper Monkey brings DTrace debugging capabilities to JavaScript
JavaScript was becoming critically important but remained poorly understood and difficult to debug
The tool represents a major leap forward for web development debugging
money_quotes: Brendan's work is an indisputable quantum leap for what has become one of the most important, one of the most misunderstood, and certainly one of the most undebuggable platforms on the planet: JavaScript.
If you do anything AJAX-ian that's evenly vaguely performance or footprint sensitive, you're wasting your time if you're not using Helper Monkey.

Adam's line was that Brendan had just propelled JavaScript debugging forward by '100,000 years' – which is not to say that Helper Monkey is like debugging in the 1021st century, but rather that debugging JavaScript without Helper Monkey is like debugging in the late Pleistocene.
posts.key_insight: JavaScript had become critically important infrastructure while remaining essentially impossible to debug properly, making tools like Helper Monkey that bring serious observability to the platform genuinely transformative.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces Ian Murdock's hiring at Sun Microsystems, expressing enthusiasm about what this means for OpenSolaris. He praises Murdock's pragmatic approach to technology and his past support of OpenSolaris, particularly his defense against licensing FUD. Cantrill sees this as a major step toward combining the best of Debian's package management with Solaris's technical capabilities.
key_points: Ian Murdock, founder of Debian, has joined Sun Microsystems
Murdock previously defended OpenSolaris against licensing FUD from the Debian community
This hire represents progress toward combining Debian-style package management with OpenSolaris/DTrace
Cantrill hints at undisclosed work his team has been doing for the past year
money_quotes: I have always been impressed with Ian's decidedly pragmatic views of technology.
He took a decisive and courageous stand against some absurd Debian-borne anti-Nexenta licensing FUD.
Ian's arrival at Sun is a huge lurch forward towards the wide-spread productization of this ideal.
It looks like we're going to be able to apt-get our cake, and DTrace it too!
posts.key_insight: Ian Murdock joining Sun represents a bridge between the Debian and OpenSolaris communities that could bring user-friendly package management to enterprise-grade systems software.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on the outsized influence of Brown University's CS169 operating systems course on Silicon Valley, noting that all three DTrace creators and seven key contributors to Solaris 10's revolutionary technologies (DTrace, ZFS, SMF, FMA, Zones) came from this single course taught by Tom Doeppner over thirty years. The post announces a symposium at Brown honoring DTrace and former CS169 TAs, where Cantrill presented on 'the inculcation of systems thinking.' He hints at deeper thoughts on systems education while sharing that his actual talk ranged from Soviet aircraft to Ruby stack traces.
key_points: All three DTrace creators came from Brown's CS169 operating systems course
Seven CS169 alumni at Sun played major roles in Solaris 10's key technologies
The course has produced top engineers at NetApp, SGI, Adobe, VMware and many startups
Tom Doeppner has taught CS169 for thirty years, creating a legendary program
Brown hosted a symposium honoring DTrace and CS169's contributions to industry
money_quotes: This course has been taught by the same professor, Tom Doeppner, over its thirty year lifetime, and has become something of a legend in Silicon Valley, having produced some of the top engineers at major companies like NetApp, SGI, Adobe, and VMware – not to mention tons of smaller companies.
At Sun, CS169 has cast a particularly long shadow, with seven CS169 alums having together played major roles in developing many of the revolutionary technologies in Solaris 10.
Seizing the opportunity for intellectual indulgence, I chose to reflect on a broad topic: the inculcation of systems thinking.
posts.key_insight: A single rigorous operating systems course, taught consistently by one professor over decades, can produce an extraordinary concentration of talent that shapes major technologies across the industry.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces that Joyent has launched a dedicated DTrace site to help Ruby on Rails developers with performance monitoring. The post highlights Joyent's work on Ruby DTrace providers and promotes an upcoming panel discussion on Rails scaling. It's a brief promotional piece connecting DTrace tooling to the growing Rails ecosystem.
key_points: Joyent created Ruby 1.8.5 and 1.8.6 DTrace providers
New dedicated DTrace site launched for Rails developers
Panel discussion on Rails scaling happening in San Francisco
money_quotes: If you are a Rails shop that cares about performance (and, yes, they very much exist), the resources at the Joyent page should become invaluable to you.
posts.key_insight: DTrace instrumentation was being extended into the Ruby/Rails ecosystem through Joyent's work, bringing systems-level observability to web application developers.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces the birth of his second son, Alexander Morgan Gaffikin Cantrill, born on June 17th, 2007. The post is a brief, joyful announcement noting the baby's impressive birth weight of 9 pounds, 7 ounces. Cantrill reflects humorously on how much he's forgotten about infant care in the nearly three years since his first son was born.
key_points: Alexander Morgan Gaffikin Cantrill was born June 17, 2007
The baby weighed 9 pounds, 7 ounces and arrived five days early
This is Cantrill's second child, nearly three years after his first son Tobin
money_quotes: It's amazing how much one forgets over nearly three years; I've found myself cramming on forgotten (if simple) ideas like burping and 'tummy time'.
But I can still swaddle like an all-pro!
posts.key_insight: A personal announcement celebrating new fatherhood and the humbling experience of relearning infant care basics.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill recounts Team DTrace's appearance on the Scoble Show, which took place the day after his younger son was born. The post is a lighthearted reflection on the interview, complete with celebratory cigars that led to a reprimand from building management. He also addresses the status of DTrace on the iPhone, noting that while it's not yet available on ARM, a port may be in development.
key_points: Team DTrace appeared on the Scoble Show for an interview
The interview occurred the day after Cantrill's son was born
Celebratory cigars in the office led to a conversation with building management
DTrace was not yet ported to iPhone/ARM but work may be underway
money_quotes: After the photo, we returned to our office and smoked the cigars – and then had an unplanned conversation with the building management about never again smoking cigars in the office.
I responded that I was done having kids, so they had nothing to worry about.
So the dream is alive!
posts.key_insight: Even significant technical achievements like DTrace can be celebrated with simple pleasures and humor, blending personal milestones with professional ones.

posts.summary: Cantrill reviews the book 'Beautiful Code' (to which he contributed a chapter) and reflects on the varied definitions of beauty in software. He challenges the common admiration for recursive code elegance, arguing that recursion creates dangerous implicit dependencies on architecture, data models, and compilers that can lead to stack overflows. Using Rob Pike's globbing code as an example, he demonstrates how 'beautiful' recursive code can fail catastrophically on pathological input, with stack limits varying by an order of magnitude across platforms.
key_points: The book's greatest strength and weakness is its varied definitions of beauty
Recursive code often needs to be unrolled when facing larger-than-expected data structures
Pike's elegant globbing code overflows the stack on pathological input
Stack overflow thresholds vary by 10x across architectures and compilers
Correctness should trump aesthetic elegance in production code
money_quotes: The book's greatest strength – and, frankly, its greatest weakness – is that the chapters are so incredibly varied.
If Oprah were a code cranking propeller head, this would be the ideal choice for her book club.
It leaves you with edge conditions that on the one hand really are edge conditions (deeply pathological input), but with a failure mode (a stack overflow) that's just too nasty to ignore.
For most aesthetic senses, none of these options is going to make the code more beautiful – but they will make it indisputably more correct.
While recursion is a beautiful technique, it is one that ends up with the ugliest of implicit dependencies: on the CPU architecture, on the data model and on the compiler.
While recursion is still beautiful to me personally, it will always be a beauty that is more superficial than profound.
posts.key_insight: Elegant recursive code carries hidden dangers because its correctness depends on implicit platform-specific constraints like stack size, making such beauty more superficial than profound in production systems.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on the book 'Beautiful Code' and why its subjective title is perfect for a collection with wildly divergent notions of ideal software. He argues that software engineering suffers from being viewed as mundane labor rather than a unique craft at the intersection of mathematics and engineering. The book's value lies not in presenting a unified vision of beauty, but in helping readers develop their own aesthetic sense through exposure to many different approaches.
key_points: Software is unique because 'the blueprints are the thing; the abstraction is the machine'
Programming language choice ultimately affects thought and aesthetic sensibility
The book contains chapters with nearly mutually exclusive definitions of beauty
Reading diverse code helps developers discover and refine their own voice
money_quotes: We suffer -- tremendously -- from a bias from traditional engineering that writing code is like digging a ditch: that it is a mundane activity best left to day labor -- and certainly beneath the Gentleman Engineer.
Software is not like a dam or a superhighway or a power plant: in software, the blueprints _are_ the thing; the abstraction _is_ the machine.
It is long past time that our discipline stood on its feet, stepped out from the shadow of sooty 19th century disciplines, and embraced our unique confluence between mathematics and engineering.
It's hard to imagine how one could have a sense of beauty that is so uncritical as to equally accommodate all of these languages.
You should read this book not because it's cover-to-cover perfection, but rather to hone your own sense of beauty.
posts.key_insight: The subjectivity of 'beautiful code' is a feature, not a bug—engaging with divergent aesthetic visions helps programmers discover and refine their own voice in their craft.

posts.summary: Cantrill shares a photo from OSCON that Adam Leventhal took, which apparently shows Jeff Waugh (known as 'jdub') in a compromising position regarding DTrace or its competitors. The brief post references an earlier interaction where Ubuntu and DTrace 'broke bread,' implying Waugh should have learned something from that experience. The photo is presented as a rare instance where an image captures something meaningful about the normally abstract software world.
key_points: A photo from OSCON captured something notable about software competition
Jeff Waugh (jdub) was caught doing something Cantrill finds hypocritical
References previous Ubuntu/DTrace discussions
money_quotes: Software is such an abstract domain that it is rare for a photograph to say much.
posts.key_insight: Even in the abstract world of software, sometimes a photograph can capture the irony of someone's actions contradicting their stated positions.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces that his DTrace Tech Talk at Google is now available online. He points viewers to specific timestamps for the most interesting content: a demo of DTrace for Python featuring John Levon's Python ustack helper, and a Q&A session that opened with an obvious question that made both him and the audience laugh. He adds a disclaimer that his candid answer to that question represents only his personal opinion, not Sun's official position.
key_points: DTrace Tech Talk video from Google is now publicly available
Features demonstration of DTrace integration with Python
John Levon developed the Python ustack helper capability
Q&A session included a question obvious enough to provoke laughter from the entire room
money_quotes: If you've seen me present before and you don't want to suffer through the same tired anecdotes, arcane jokes, and disturbing rants, jump ahead to about 57:16 to see a demo of DTrace for Python.
Any guesses what the first question was? (Hint: it was such an obvious question, both I and the room erupted with laughter.)
Note that my rather candid answer to that question represents my opinion alone, and does not constitute legal advice – and nor does it represent any sort of official position of Sun.
posts.key_insight: DTrace's integration with dynamic languages like Python, demonstrated through John Levon's ustack helper, showcased the tool's expanding relevance beyond traditional systems programming.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill responds to NetApp's lawsuit against Sun over ZFS, disputing NetApp CEO Dave Hitz's claim that Sun initiated patent negotiations seeking money. He argues that NetApp actually approached Sun (via STK) first, and criticizes NetApp for filing in the patent troll-friendly East Texas jurisdiction and for having a history of aggressive patent litigation after acquiring patents from bankrupt companies. Cantrill sarcastically offers to help NetApp port DTrace to their platform instead of pursuing litigation.
key_points: NetApp sued Sun over ZFS patents but misrepresented how the dispute began
NetApp filed in East Texas, known as a haven for patent trolls
NetApp has a history of buying patents from bankrupt companies and suing competitors
Sun's disorganization actually prevents it from being systematically evil
money_quotes: I have always said that a major advantage of working for or doing business with Sun is that we're too disorganized to be evil.

Being disorganized causes lots of problems, but actively doing evil isn't among them; approaching NetApp to extract gobs of money shows, if nothing else, a level of organization that we as a company are rarely able to summon.
I'm not a lawyer, but we're both headquartered in California – let's duke this out in California like grown-ups.

To me, the misrepresentation of how the suit came to be, coupled with the choice of venue and history show that NetApp – despite their claptrap to the contrary – would prefer to meet their competition in a court of law than in the marketplace.
After all, everyone deserves DTrace – even patent trolls.
posts.key_insight: Companies that choose to compete through patent litigation rather than innovation reveal their true character through their choice of venue and their willingness to misrepresent events.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that the business case for open source should not focus on 'how does this directly help me?' but rather 'how much does it cost me?' and 'does it hurt me?' Using DTrace's inclusion in Mac OS X Leopard as a case study, he demonstrates that open sourcing technology costs essentially nothing (since software has no variable cost), doesn't hurt Sun (since Mac OS X and Solaris aren't direct competitors), and actually helps by expanding the technology's reach in unanticipated ways. The key insight is that open source transforms a product feature into a technological advance that transcends any single company.
key_points: Open source business questions should shift from 'how does this help me?' to 'how much does it cost me?' and 'does it hurt me?'
Software's most beautiful property is that it has no variable cost - only fixed costs matter
The real choice isn't between giving away or selling technology, but between a potential customer using your technology versus someone else's
Open source benefits are often unanticipatable - no one at Sun predicted DTrace would benefit from Ruby/Rails developers on Mac OS X
Technology presence in multiple systems transforms it from a product feature into a technological advance
money_quotes: The thrust of the business questions around open source should not be 'how does this directly help me?' or 'can't I sell them something instead?' but rather 'how much does it cost me?' and 'does it hurt me?'

The choice is not between 'I give a customer my technology' and 'I sell a customer my technology', but rather between 'a customer that I never would have had uses my technology' and 'a customer that I never would have had uses someone else's technology.'
This is possible because of that most beautiful property of software: it has no variable cost; the only meaningful costs associated with software are fixed costs.
It has cost Sun more money in terms of my time to blog how this didn't cost anything to Sun than it did in fact cost Sun in the first place.
The presence of DTrace in other systems assures that it transcends a company or its products – that it moves beyond a mere feature, and becomes a technological advance.
This all serves as an object lesson of how unanticipatable the benefits of open source can be.
posts.key_insight: Open source strategy should be evaluated not by direct revenue impact but by its negligible cost, minimal competitive harm, and potential to transform proprietary technology into industry-standard advances that benefit the originator through broader adoption and ecosystem growth.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on how a chance reading of a Byte magazine article about QNX in 1993 led to formative summer internships at the company, and announces that DTrace is being ported to QNX. The post weaves together personal history, the announcement of the port, and a tribute to the late Dan Hildebrand, who mentored Cantrill during his time at QNX. The announcement serves as both a professional milestone and a bittersweet reminder of mentors lost.
key_points: A Byte magazine article about QNX inspired Cantrill to cold-email for a summer job, which he got
DTrace is being ported to QNX by Colin Burgess and Thomas Fletcher
The port was the mystery 'other system' Cantrill alluded to in his Google talk
Dan Hildebrand, who gave Cantrill his start, passed away and is remembered fondly
money_quotes: There are moments in anyone's life that seem mundane at the time, but become pivotal in retrospect: that chance meeting of your spouse, or the job you applied for on a lark, or the inspirational course that you just stumbled upon.
Some speculated that this 'surely' must be Windows – so hopefully the fact that it was QNX will serve to remind that it's a big heterogeneous world out there.
I would love nothing more right now than to call up danh, chat about the exciting prospects for DTrace on QNX, and reminisce about the many conversations over our shared cube wall so long ago.
posts.key_insight: Pivotal career moments often emerge from seemingly mundane chances taken—a magazine read in an airport, a cold email sent on a whim—and the mentors who respond to those chances leave lasting marks even after they're gone.

posts.summary: Cantrill critiques Scott Rosenberg's 'Dreaming in Code' for framing software development primarily through the lens of difficulty rather than understanding software's unique nature as a confluence of information and machine. He argues that Rosenberg's choice to follow the doomed Chandler project led to an overly pessimistic view that misses software's most profound quality: its ability to achieve timeless perfection. Unlike physical structures that decay, software can become permanent infrastructure that future generations build upon, with each generation working at higher levels of abstraction.
key_points: Software is unique as a confluence of information and machine, not merely 'hard to build'
Rosenberg chose a doomed project (Chandler) which biased his conclusions about software development
Software can achieve absolute, timeless perfection unlike physical engineering
Successful software sediments into infrastructure, enabling future abstraction layers
To understand software development properly, one should study systems programmers building databases, operating systems, and VMs
money_quotes: If you insist on looking at the world through a bucket of shit, the world starts to look an awful lot like a bucket of shit.
Software – unlike everything else that we build – can achieve a timeless and absolute perfection.
Long after the Lighthouse at Alexandria crumbled, Euclid's greatest common divisor algorithm is showing no signs of wearing out.

Once software achieves something approximating perfection (and a surprising amount of it does), it sediments into the information infrastructure: the abstractions defined by the software become the bedrock that future generations may build upon.
PC-era zillionaire with too many ideas funds on-the-beach programmers to change the world with software that has no revenue model. Yikes – call me when you ship something…
posts.key_insight: Software's true nature is not that it's merely difficult to build, but that it uniquely can achieve permanent perfection and sediment into foundational infrastructure that elevates all future work.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on how boom/bust cycles are endemic across industries, from his grandfather's petroleum engineering career derailed by the oil bust to his own experience in software during the Dot-Com crash. He initially thought software would be 'bust-proof' since it wasn't tied to physical resources, but learned that tech cycles are if anything more extreme. Despite this, he still prefers software's volatility to other industries since at least there aren't literal ghost towns yet.
key_points: Boom/bust cycles are endemic across industries, from oil to mining to software
The author naively believed software would be 'bust-proof' since it wasn't tied to physical commodities
The Dot-Com bust proved tech cycles can be even more extreme than resource-based industries
Software companies can rise and fall faster than those in traditional industries
money_quotes: So when, in the early 1990s, I realized that my life's calling was in software, I felt a sense of relief: here was an industry that was at last not shackled to the fickle Earth – an industry that was surely 'bust-proof' at some level. Ha!
As I learned (painfully, like most everyone else) in the Dot-Com boom and subsequent bust, booms and busts are – if anything – even more endemic in our industry.
Companies grow from nothing to spectacular heights in virtually no time at all – and can crash back into nothingness just as quickly.
I'll still take our boom and bust cycles over those in other industries: there are, after all, still not quite yet software 'ghost towns'
posts.key_insight: Software's intangibility doesn't insulate it from boom/bust cycles – if anything, the lack of physical constraints makes tech companies rise and fall even faster than traditional industries.

posts.summary: Cantrill announces the first-ever DTrace unconference, dtrace.conf(08), to be held in San Francisco in March 2008. The event is motivated by the growing DTrace ecosystem including ports to new systems, language providers, and new tools. He highlights the unique nature of the DTrace community as systems generalists who think both abstractly and concretely about systems.
key_points: First DTrace unconference announced for March 14, 2008 in San Francisco
DTrace ecosystem expanding with ports, language providers, and new tools
DTrace community characterized as systems generalists with abstract and concrete thinking
money_quotes: Because DTrace operates both across abstraction boundaries and to great depth, it naturally attracts technologists who do the same – systems generalists who couple an ability to think abstractly about the system with a thirst for the concrete data necessary to better understand it.
posts.key_insight: DTrace's ability to work across abstraction layers attracts a unique community of systems generalists who combine abstract thinking with a hunger for concrete data.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill recaps dtrace.conf(08), an unconference that brought together 70 software engineers working across the entire technology stack, from FPGAs to high-level languages like Erlang. The conference featured live demos on production machines and Mac laptops, showcasing DTrace's ability to bridge different layers of the software stack. Cantrill emphasizes the unique cross-pollination of talent at the event, where dinner conversations naturally mixed people from different companies and technology domains.
key_points: DTrace cuts across vertical boundaries in the software stack, from PHP through to the kernel
Conference covered everything from FPGAs to VMs to operating systems to high-level languages
Nearly every presentation included a live demo, many on production machines or Mac laptops
The Leopard port opened new vistas for DTrace on a popular development platform
Open sourcing DTrace has been in Sun's best business interest
Cross-domain mixing at dinner showed the unique community-building power of the event
money_quotes: DTrace is able to cut across vertical boundaries in the software stack (taking you from say, PHP through to the kernel).

As it is the nature of DTrace to reflect not the flowery theory of a system but rather its brutish reality, it was not at all surprising (but refreshing nonetheless) that nearly every presentation had a demo to go with it.

I claimed that the room had as much software talent as has ever been assembled in 70 people; after seeing the day's presentations and the many discussions that they spawned, I would double down on that claim in an instant.

It almost brought a tear to the eye to see such interaction among such superlative software talent from such disjoint domains, and my dinner partner captured the zeitgeist precisely: 'it's like a commune in here.'
posts.key_insight: The greatest value of a technology community conference lies not just in the technical content but in the cross-pollination that occurs when talented people from different layers of the stack come together around a unifying tool.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill reflects on attending a tribute for Jim Gray at Berkeley, honoring the legendary computer scientist who disappeared in 2007. Despite never meeting Gray personally, Cantrill was deeply inspired by Gray's rare combination of brilliant intellect and genuine emotional engagement with collaborators. The tribute revealed how Gray balanced visionary thinking with practical implementation throughout his career, from System R to TerraServer, always insisting on building real systems while pursuing big ideas.
key_points: Jim Gray possessed a rare combination of intellectual brilliance and deep emotional engagement with collaborators
Gray balanced theoretical vision with practical implementation - writing papers while also cutting code
His work spanned from transaction processing fundamentals to pioneering database benchmarks to web-scale systems
Computer science too often suffers from being either detached from reality or drowning in it
money_quotes: It's rare for a gifted mind to be emotionally engaged with others (they are often 'anti-human' in the words of one presenter), but practically unique for an intellect of Jim's caliber to have his ability for intense, collaborative engagement with so many others.
All along, Jim remained committed to big, future-looking ideas, while still insisting on designing and implementing actual systems in the present.
We in computer science do not strike that balance often enough – we are too often either detached from reality, or drowning in it.
I found myself wondering if Jim didn't belong in the pantheon of Erdős and Feynman – two other larger-than-life figures that shared Jim's zeal for problems and affection for those solving them.
posts.key_insight: The rarest and most valuable quality in computer science is the ability to remain committed to visionary ideas while still insisting on building real, working systems.

posts.summary: Cantrill responds to renewed Linux kernel community discussions criticizing SystemTap's usability compared to DTrace. He argues that DTrace's superiority isn't accidental but stems from deliberate architectural decisions in its foundational components like CTF, DOF, and provider-specified stability. He contends that SystemTap developers don't fully appreciate these design problems, which is why companies like Apple, QNX, and FreeBSD chose to port DTrace rather than build alternatives. He highlights Paul Fox's nascent DTrace port to Linux as the more realistic path forward.
key_points: DTrace's usability comes from deliberate design decisions, not just good implementation
Foundational innovations like CTF, DOF, and provider-specified stability enable DTrace's ease of use
SystemTap doesn't appreciate the scope of problems DTrace solves
Apple, QNX, and FreeBSD all concluded that porting DTrace is the only way to achieve parity
Paul Fox's DTrace Linux port represents a more realistic path than SystemTap
money_quotes: I find it a bit disappointing that no one in the discussion seems to realize that DTrace 'just works' not merely my implementation, but also by design.
Over and over again, we made architectural and technical design decisions that would yield an instrumentation framework that would be not just safe, powerful and flexible, but also usable.
The subtle bit here is that many of those decisions were not at the surface of the system (where the discussion on the Linux list seems to be currently mired), but in its guts.
The only way to have a system at parity with DTrace is to port DTrace.
posts.key_insight: Software that 'just works' is the result of deliberate architectural decisions made deep in the system's foundations, not surface-level features—and knockoffs that don't understand these foundational choices will never achieve parity.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that the computing industry fails to study its failures, unlike traditional engineering fields, and uses Robert Colwell's analysis of the Intel 432 as a prime example of valuable failure analysis. The 432 was a disastrous microprocessor that combined untested capability-based architecture with terrible execution—lacking registers, caches, and immediate values beyond 0 and 1. While the object model was blamed for its failure, Colwell's paper reveals the performance problems stemmed from implementation 'artifacts' caused by rigid adherence to novel concepts at the expense of fundamentals. Cantrill draws parallels to modern hardware transactional memory efforts, warning that hardware innovations requiring significant system software support risk repeating the 432's mistakes.
key_points: Computing systems lack a culture of studying failures, unlike traditional engineering disciplines
The Intel 432's failure was caused by both untested ideas and terrible execution, not just its novel object model
The 432 lacked fundamental features (registers, caches, immediate values) because resources went to the object model
Poor compiler implementation made every function call a context switch, costing 25-35% performance
Hardware transactional memory faces similar risks of disconnect between hardware innovation and software requirements
money_quotes: To understand systems, you must understand their pathologies – systems are most instructive when they fail.

Despite the fact that failure in our domain can be every bit as expensive (if not more so) than in traditional engineering domains, our failures do not (usually) involve loss of life or physical property and there is thus little public demand for us to study them – and a tremendous industrial bias for us to forget them as much and as quickly as possible.

The upshot of these decisions is that you have more code (because you have no immediates) accessing more memory (because you have no registers) that is dog-slow (because you have no data cache) that itself is not cached (because you have no instruction cache). Yee haw!
Intel designers believed that 0 and 1 'would cover nearly all the need for constants', a conclusion that Colwell (generously) describes as 'almost certainly in error.'

Its performance was 'dominated, in large part, by artifacts and not by concepts.' If there's one imperfection with Colwell's work, it's that he doesn't realize how convincingly he's made the case that these artifacts were induced by a rigid and foolish adherence to the concepts.
posts.key_insight: When engineers become so enamored with novel concepts that they sacrifice fundamental requirements (like caches and registers), the resulting 'artifacts' of poor implementation become indistinguishable from flaws in the concepts themselves.

posts.summary: Cantrill humorously critiques the launch of Cuil, a hyped Google competitor from 2008, by pointing out the absurd image associations their search engine returned for 'dtrace' queries. Despite his stated policy of not publicly mocking others' software bugs, he makes an exception for Cuil's comically wrong results, which included images of quilts, strip malls, and the Palisades Interstate Parkway. The post serves as a gentle takedown of overhyped technology that fails to deliver on basic functionality.
key_points: Cuil was heavily hyped as a potential Google competitor in 2008
The search engine returned bizarre, unrelated images alongside search results
Even basic searches like 'dtrace' produced comically wrong visual associations
The post demonstrates how overhyped technology often fails at fundamentals
money_quotes: In general, I don't believe in drawing attention to bugs in the software of others: any significant body of software is likely to have bugs, and I think one can too easily draw overly broad inferences by looking at software through the lens of its defects.
When a new search engine pops up, my egotistical reflex is to first search for 'dtrace'.

I can't say for certain that I've never travelled on the Palisades Interstate Parkway, but with all deference to that 38.25-mile stretch of tarmac, I do believe that I can say that it played no role in DTrace – or DTrace in it.
posts.key_insight: Overhyped technology competitors often fail spectacularly at basic functionality, and sometimes the bugs are so absurd they warrant breaking one's own rule against public criticism.

posts.summary: Cantrill recounts the harrowing final 24 hours before DTrace integrated into Solaris on September 3, 2003, when the team discovered a critical boot failure on legacy UltraSPARC-I hardware. Through systematic debugging, they traced the problem not to their architectural changes but to an accidental exclusion of a critical I/O line when folding old tracing code under a DEBUG-only define. The post celebrates DTrace's fifth birthday by sharing previously unpublished historical documents, including the original 1999 presentation and the integration announcement email.
key_points: DTrace took nearly two years to develop and pushed all three engineers to their absolute limits
The boot failure was traced to a cosmetic change that accidentally made critical I/O code DEBUG-only
The DEBUG variant working while non-DEBUG failed was the key clue to solving the mystery
Core DTrace concepts like production instrumentation and zero disabled probe effect existed from 1999, but the D language came later
money_quotes: DTrace was a project that extended all three of us to our absolute limit as software engineers – and the 24 hours before integration was then (and remains now) the most harrowing of my career.

This is an awful failure mode because with no debugger and no fatal error, one has no place to start other than to start adding print statements – or start ripping out the code that is the difference between the working system and the busted one.

This was an incredibly important discovery: while we had a bevy of architectural changes that could conceivably be invalid on an ancient CPU, we had no such HBA-specific changes – this was more likely to be something marring the surface of our work rather than cracking its foundation.
We hadn't seen this until now because these drivers were only used on ancient machines – machines on which we had never tested non-DEBUG.

We fixed the problem, and all of our machines booted DEBUG and non-DEBUG – and we felt like we were breathing again for the first time in the more than six hours that we had been working on the problem.
posts.key_insight: Even the most carefully architected systems software can be derailed by seemingly cosmetic changes, and systematic debugging under extreme pressure requires both methodical elimination and the humility to question your assumptions about where the real problem lies.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that the tech industry has a recurring pattern of concurrency 'shysters' who peddle overcomplicated solutions to exaggerated problems. Drawing parallels between the discredited two-level threading model of the 1990s and transactional memory advocacy in 2008, he contends that both promised simplicity but delivered complexity. He emphasizes that transactional memory is fundamentally unsuitable for systems software because real systems interact with external resources beyond memory, and advocates should rely on data rather than fear-mongering assertions.
key_points: The two-level threading model was promoted as a solution to SMP challenges but proved fundamentally flawed and was eventually eliminated from Solaris
Transactional memory advocates use fear-mongering about concurrency without providing data or evidence from actual systems experience
System software cannot use transactional memory because it interacts with external resources (I/O devices, kernels, firmware) that exist outside memory
CMP arose from the success of concurrent software, not the failure of hardware imagination
Solutions that claim simplicity but export complexity to programmers are doomed to fail
money_quotes: There also seemed to be no end of concurrency hucksters and shysters, each eager to peddle their own quack cure for the miasma.
The fact that an engineer could speak so honestly spoke volumes for both the engineer and the company.
No data, no qualifiers, no study, no rationale, no evidence of experience trying to develop such systems – just a naked assertion used as a prop for a complicated and dubious solution.
There remains a fatal flaw with transactional memory: much system software can never be in a transaction because it does not merely operate on memory.
For me at least, transactional memory is an unacceptable solution to a non-problem.
It was the success of concurrent software – and not the failure of imagination on the part of hardware designers – that gave rise to the early CMP implementations.
posts.key_insight: Concurrency problems repeatedly attract snake-oil solutions that promise simplicity but deliver complexity, and practitioners should demand data and real-world evidence rather than accepting fear-based assertions about programming difficulty.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill recounts the origin story of Fishworks, a project he co-founded with Mike Shapiro at Sun Microsystems in 2005 to build an integrated NAS appliance. Despite Sun having all the necessary components (ZFS, DTrace, NFS), they had zero market share in NAS storage. After pitching the idea to Sun's CTO Greg Papadopoulos and getting unexpected immediate support, the team built what became the Sun Storage 7000 series, featuring DTrace-based analytics that visually answer questions about storage performance.
key_points: Sun had revolutionary technologies but zero NAS market share due to organizational barriers
The infrastructure needed for special-purpose appliances turned out to be general-purpose across different applications
DTrace-based analytics were designed to answer questions in terms of system abstractions rather than implementation details
Visual interfaces can serve as a 'force multiplier' for underlying instrumentation technology
money_quotes: Despite having invented network attached storage with NFS in 1983, and despite having the necessary components to efficiently serve files built into the system, and despite having absolutely killer technologies like ZFS and DTrace, Sun had no share – none – of the NAS market.

At that instant, we realized that the NAS problem was but one example of a larger problem, and that the infrastructure to build fully-integrated, special-purpose systems was itself general-purpose across those special purposes!
Greg rose from his chair and exclaimed, 'let's do it!' Mike and I were caught a bit flat-footed; we had expected a much safer, more traditional answer – like 'let's commission a task force!'
With analytics, we sought to harness the great power of DTrace: its ability to answer ad hoc questions that are phrased in terms of the system's abstractions instead of its implementation.
We have come up with ways to visualize and interact with data that actually function as a force multiplier for the underlying instrumentation technology.
posts.key_insight: Having best-in-class technology components is insufficient without organizational willingness to integrate them into complete products that solve real customer problems.

posts.summary: Cantrill recounts how he initially made a poor decision to extend a simple Perl script into a full command-line interface for Sun's Fishworks storage appliance, only to discover that Perl was fundamentally unsuited for software-in-the-large. After painful months of struggling with the language, he rewrote the shell as a JavaScript/C hybrid, which not only solved the immediate problems but enabled new functionality like a full scripting environment. The post serves as a cautionary tale about not questioning initial tool choices when problems grow, and demonstrates how comprehensive test suites can actually accelerate rewrites.
key_points: Don't assume your initial tool choice remains correct as the problem grows
When facing a decision between two paths, implement common elements first to defer and inform the decision
Comprehensive test suites can accelerate rewrites by providing ready-made validation
The right architecture often makes new functionality 'fall out' naturally
Browser-based interfaces need complementary CLI modalities for troubleshooting and automation
money_quotes: Perl is an undesigned dung heap of a language entirely inappropriate for software in-the-large.

There's no worse feeling for a software engineer: knowing that you have made what is likely the wrong decision, but feeling that you can't revisit that decision because of the time already spent down the wrong path.
When faced with a decision, determine if there are elements that are common to both paths, and implement them first, thereby deferring the decision.
Contrary to the impressions of some about test-driven development, the presence of tests actually accelerated the development of the new shell tremendously.

As often happens when the right software architecture is in place, the further I went down the path of the JavaScript/C hybrid, the more often I had the experience of new, interesting functionality simply falling out.
posts.key_insight: When a problem grows beyond your initial solution, the sunk cost of the wrong path is always less than the ongoing cost of continuing down it—and a good test suite transforms rewrites from risky gambles into confident migrations.

posts.summary: Brendan Gregg discovered that loud noises can cause measurable disk latency in datacenter hard drives, initially stumbled upon while investigating latency caused by a missing screw on a drive bracket. The discovery was documented in a video demonstration and analyzed using Fishworks analytics. This connects to known research on non-repeatable runout (NRRO) in hard drives, where noise can interfere with disk head positioning.
key_points: Loud noises can cause measurable latency in spinning hard drives
Discovery originated from investigating a missing screw causing drive vibration
Fishworks analytics enabled visualization of the latency patterns
Non-repeatable runout (NRRO) is a known phenomenon caused by noise interference
money_quotes: This may seem silly, but it's not farfetched: Brendan actually made this discovery while exploring drive latency that he had seen in a lab machine due to a missing screw on a drive bracket.
As Jon notes, noise is a known cause of what is called a non-repeatable runout (NRRO) – though it's unclear if Brendan's shouting is exactly the kind of noise-induced NRRO that Jon had in mind…
posts.key_insight: Physical phenomena like sound vibrations can have measurable effects on disk performance, and good observability tools can help uncover unexpected sources of system latency.

posts.summary: Cantrill discovers that NetApp's original 1992 business plan explicitly targeted Sun Microsystems with the same differentiators—high availability, easy administration, high performance, and low price—that Sun's Fishworks team is now using to target NetApp. The irony is that the company NetApp disrupted is now disrupting NetApp using nearly the same playbook. Cantrill argues this illustrates a fundamental truth: economics always wins, and no company can sustain itself against unfavorable economic forces indefinitely.
key_points: NetApp's 1992 business plan specifically targeted Sun as a slower, more expensive competitor
NetApp's criticisms of Sun then mirror Sun's Fishworks criticisms of NetApp now
The same disruption playbook is being used against the original disruptor
Economic forces ultimately determine technology outcomes regardless of current market position
money_quotes: It is stunning the degree to which NetApp's own business plan – nearly verbatim – is being used against it, not least by the very company that NetApp originally disrupted.

NetApp's success with their original business plan and our nascent success with Fishworks point to the most important lesson that the history of technology has to teach: economics always wins – a product or a technology or a company ultimately cannot prop up unsustainable economics.

Perhaps unlike Hitz, however, I had to learn that lesson the hard way: in the post-bubble meltdown that brought Sun within an inch of its life. But then again, perhaps Hitz has yet to have his final lesson on the subject…
The culture among software engineers at Sun places little value on performance.
posts.key_insight: Market disruptors eventually become the disrupted, as the economic forces that enabled their rise can be wielded against them by hungrier competitors using the same playbook.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that SPEC SFS, the dominant NAS benchmark, has become completely divorced from reality and actively harms customers by rewarding the wrong engineering decisions. The benchmark's ancient workload mix (based on 1986 data), arbitrary scaling rules that eliminate caching benefits, and lack of price disclosure combine to encourage vendors to build absurdly expensive, short-stroked disk configurations instead of innovative caching architectures. Rather than measuring useful performance, SPEC SFS has devolved into a dragster race where vendors compete on absolute performance at any cost, concealing economic insanity from customers.
key_points: SPEC SFS workload is based on data from 1986 and hasn't meaningfully changed, ignoring the massive growth in client-side DRAM caching
The scaling rule that ties working set size to operations per second eliminates the benefits of intelligent caching architectures
Vendors short-stroke thousands of 15K RPM drives to win benchmarks, creating configurations no sane customer would buy
The lack of price disclosure conceals that benchmarked configurations cost $384/GB - 8X more expensive than DRAM
SPEC SFS 2008 made everything worse with a 40X increase in working set size, ensuring flash can't compete with disk farms
money_quotes: SPEC SFS has decayed to the point that it is serving the opposite ends: by rewarding the wrong engineering decisions, punishing the right ones and eliminating price from the discussion, SPEC SFS has actually led to lower performing, more expensive systems!
Short-stroking 224 15K RPM drives is the equivalent of fueling a dragster with nitromethane – it is top performance at a price so high as to be useless off the dragstrip.
The decision to both increase the scaling rule and increase the working set ratio is so terrible that incompetence becomes indistinguishable from malice.
I recently interviewed a prospective engineer who was not yet born when this data was gathered – and I've always thought it wise to be wary of data older than oneself.
This embodies the essence of the failings of SPEC SFS: the (mis)design of the benchmark demands economic insanity – but the lack of pricing disclosure conceals that insanity from the casual observer.
We have, after all, designed our systems to be run, not just to be sold.
posts.key_insight: Benchmarks without price disclosure create perverse incentives where vendors optimize for meaningless metrics at absurd costs, ultimately harming the customers they claim to serve.

posts.summary: Cantrill dismantles NetApp engineer Mike Eisler's defense of SPEC SFS benchmark's lack of pricing disclosure, arguing that claims about opaque pricing benefiting customers and component costs falling too fast to track are absurd rationalizations. He coins the term 'Moore's Outlaws' to describe enterprise storage vendors who use outdated components while claiming technology curves justify their pricing opacity. The post argues that pricing disclosure is essential for benchmark credibility and that storage vendors' configurations don't reflect what customers actually purchase.
key_points: SPEC SFS benchmark lacks pricing disclosure, unlike TPC and SPC benchmarks
NetApp's defense of opaque pricing as benefiting customers is logically absurd
Enterprise storage vendors use outdated components while invoking Moore's Law to justify pricing opacity
Drive latency hasn't improved due to physical constraints (15K RPM ceiling), undermining technology curve arguments
Benchmark configurations don't correspond to systems customers actually deploy
money_quotes: Someone should let the buyers know how great they have it – those silly buyers don't seem to realize that the endless haggling over software licensing and support contracts is for them!

The single-core Mobile Celeron that NetApp currently ships in their FAS2020 and FAS2050 – a CPU with a criminally small 256K of L2 cache – is best described as a Moore's Outlaw: a CPU that, even when it first shipped six years ago, was off the curve.
It's no wonder that NetApp sees plummeting component costs when they're able to source their CPUs by dumpster diving.
We all understand that prices reflect a moment in time, and if natural inflation does not dissuade us from price disclosures, nor should any technology-induced deflation.
SPEC SFS needs pricing disclosures. TPC has them, SPC has them – and SFS needs them if the benchmark has any aspiration to enduring relevance.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise storage vendors invoke technology improvement curves to justify pricing opacity while simultaneously shipping outdated components that violate those very curves - a hypocrisy that benchmark pricing disclosures would expose.

posts.summary: Cantrill traces his journey from dismissing the ACM as irrelevant to practitioners to becoming an advocate for the organization after witnessing its transformation through ACM Queue and the revitalized Communications of the ACM. Initially viewing the ACM as lost in academic abstraction, he was won over by Queue's focus on practical engineering content. The merger of Queue into CACM's Practice section represents a broader organizational shift to include practitioners alongside academics, creating a publication that serves both communities.
key_points: ACM Queue proved that practitioner-focused content could succeed within the traditionally academic ACM
The old Communications of the ACM was so disconnected that even past ACM presidents admitted they couldn't read it
The new CACM bridges academic research and practical engineering, benefiting both communities
Software engineering as a profession lacks the formal professional responsibility expectations found in law, medicine, and traditional engineering
money_quotes: CACM was broadly viewed within the ACM as being woefully out of touch with both academic and practitioner alike, with one past president confessing that he himself couldn't stomach reading it – even when his name was on the masthead.

As professionals, I believe that we have a responsibility to our craft: to learn from our peers, to offer whatever we might have to teach, and to generally leave the profession better than we found it.

In other professions – in law, in medicine, and in more traditional engineering domains – this professional responsibility is indoctrinated to the point of expectation. But our discipline perhaps shows its youth in our ignorance of this kind of professional service.

The ACM now aspires to be the organization that represents all computer scientists – not just those who teach students, perform research and write papers, but also those of us who cut code, deliver product and deploy systems for a living.
The ACM has given us practitioners a new voice – but it is only good if we use it!
posts.key_insight: Software engineering's maturation as a profession requires practitioners to engage with professional organizations, and the ACM's transformation shows that historically academic institutions can successfully embrace those who build production systems.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill memorializes John Birrell, a FreeBSD developer who passed away after a stroke on November 20, 2009. Birrell was the first to port DTrace to FreeBSD, a significant technical achievement that required mastering both DTrace internals and the target operating system. Cantrill emphasizes Birrell's exceptional tenacity—his willingness to tackle unnecessary but correct technical challenges, and his persistence through frustrating licensing obstacles that required significant rework.
key_points: John Birrell was the first to port DTrace to FreeBSD, a significant technical undertaking
Birrell went beyond requirements by also porting the cyclic subsystem for completeness
When licensing issues arose, Birrell found creative solutions requiring additional technical work
His legacy will benefit future generations even if they never know his name
money_quotes: I have long believed that the mark of a great engineer is not how frequently they get knocked down, but rather how quickly they get back up – and in this regard, John was indisputably a giant.
DTrace passes through rough country, and a port to a foreign system is a significant undertaking that requires mastery of both DTrace and (particularly) the target system.
That he undertook this additional technical challenge anyway – even when any victory would remain hidden to all but the most expert eye – says a lot about John as both an engineer and a man.
While your legacy might remain anonymous to the future generations that will benefit from the fruits of your long labor, we will always know that it never would have happened without you.
