money_quotes: A singular point of pride for me personally is how much DTrace was used to implement and debug ZFS: Matt has used DTrace as much as anyone, and anything that he feels he needs to make his life easier is something that will almost certainly benefit all DTrace users.
DTrace itself is entirely open source, so 'OpenDTrace' is something of a redundancy.

Just to save you the time filling out your Cantrill Presentation Bingo Card: you can find me punching myself at 16:19, me offering unsolicited personal medical history at 20:11, and me getting trolled by unikernels at 38:25.
posts.key_insight: The DTrace community continues to evolve the tool through language improvements, cross-platform collaboration via OpenDTrace, and expanding instrumentation capabilities across modern languages and massive enterprise systems.

posts.summary: Cantrill discovered a mysterious tweet posted from his account that he didn't create, initially assuming he'd been hacked. After investigating the tweet's metadata, he found it contained media from a BBC journalist's tweet about Berlin, with no apparent malicious intent. He concludes this wasn't a hack but likely a software bug—either in Twitter's client or server—and leaves the tweet up as evidence for debugging.
key_points: A tweet appeared on Cantrill's account that he didn't post, triggering immediate security response
The tweet contained only images from another user's post about Berlin campaign posters with no caption or malicious content
Analysis of the tweet's JSON metadata revealed the media was sourced from a BBC editor's tweet
The lack of malicious intent and bizarre nature of the content suggests a software bug rather than a hack
Cantrill questions whether Twitter's client or server had a bug causing tweets to be created with others' media
money_quotes: There was just one problem: I didn't tweet it.
Why would someone who had just hacked my account burn it by tweeting an innocuous (if idiosyncratic) photo of campaign posters on the streets of Berlin?!
Suddenly this is feeling less like I've been hacked, and more like I'm the victim of data corruption.

I'm leaving the tweet up there in hopes that it will provide some clues – and with the belief that the villain in the story, if ever brought to justice, will be a member of the shadowy cabal that I have fought my entire career: busted software.
posts.key_insight: When something inexplicable happens with software, always consider that bugs and data corruption may be more likely culprits than malicious actors.

posts.summary: Cantrill announces Systems We Love, a new conference inspired by the Papers We Love meetup format but focused on systems rather than academic papers. He argues that while academic papers are valuable for rigorous presentation of ideas, practitioners ultimately care most about the systems themselves - the tangible embodiments of those ideas. The conference emerged from a casual Twitter inquiry that generated overwhelming enthusiasm, reflecting a broader appetite for cross-pollination between academics and practitioners in discussing technical work.
key_points: Papers We Love succeeds by broadening participants and encouraging selection of papers that appeal to heart as well as head
Academic venues for computer science papers lack the energy found in practitioner-focused meetups
Systems themselves, not just papers about them, represent the tangible embodiment of ideas
The time is right for broader conversation about how rigorous technical work is published and discussed
money_quotes: By broadening the participants and encouraging presenters to select papers that appeal to the heart as well as the head, Papers We Love has developed a singular verve.

While papers are invaluable as a mechanism for the rigorous presentation of thinking, it is ultimately the artifacts that we develop – the systems themselves – that represent the tangible embodiment of our ideas.

My keynote was fiery, and it may have landed on dry tinder: if Rik Farrow's excellent summary of my talk is any indicator, the time is right for a broader conversation about how we publish rigorous work.
Here at Joyent, a company for which love of systems is practically an organizing principle, the interest quickly boiled into a frothy fervor; we couldn't not do this!
posts.key_insight: The practitioner community is hungry for venues that celebrate systems themselves with the same passion and rigor that Papers We Love brings to academic work.

posts.summary: Cantrill encourages potential speakers to submit proposals to Systems We Love by finding genuine personal enthusiasm for a system or aspect of a system they admire. He acknowledges that experienced engineers often become cynical after dealing with 'quotidian sewage' of deployed systems, but argues there's still a core of love beneath the sedimented cynicism. The key is to keep it personal and focused on why you love something, not to lecture or assert dominance.
key_points: Proposals should begin with an implicit 'Why I love...' framing
Experienced engineers often lose their love amid daily system struggles
Software work takes us to broken boundaries rather than beautiful depths
You can love one aspect of a system while other parts enrage you
money_quotes: What catches our collective attention are systems we hate, systems that suck, systems that fail–or systems too new to know. It's refreshing to consider systems established and clever enough to love.
It can be easy to lose the love in the sluice of quotidian sewage that is a deployed system.
Beneath any sedimented cynicism there must be a core of love.
Our work as software engineers takes us to the boundaries of systems that are emerging or otherwise don't work properly rather than into the beautiful caverns deep below the surface.
It's not Systems We Know, Systems We Invented or Systems We Worship – it's Systems We Love.
posts.key_insight: The daily grind of fixing broken systems can bury our original awe and love for elegant software, but that core of enthusiasm still exists beneath the cynicism and is worth rediscovering.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on the success of the inaugural Systems We Love conference, attributing it to key design decisions: emphasizing genuine affection for technology rather than promotion, having presenters discuss others' systems rather than their own, using a single-track single-day format, double-blind selection, and assembling a diverse program committee. He argues that when 'love' becomes the unifying principle rather than specific technologies or company loyalty, it creates richer discussions among attendees with shared values but diverse experiences. The only significant issue was an overly packed schedule with 19 talks leaving insufficient break time.
key_points: Emphasizing love for technology creates irrefutable, earnest presentations free from sales undertones
Double-blind selection is essential for diversity - anything less is irresponsible
Single-track conferences create shared experiences that fuel better hallway conversations
Twenty-minute talks can achieve depth while remaining tight and allowing content variety
Program committees should be large and diverse, shooting for your dream roster
The best conferences facilitate life-changing connections between like-minded people
money_quotes: As an engineer, this makes me slightly nervous as I believe that success often teaches you nothing: luck becomes difficult to differentiate from design.

When a technologist is explaining their love for a technology, there is a level at which it's irrefutable: even if you yourself abhor the technology being described, you can't deny the presenter's affection for it.
People want to talk and hear about the things that uplift them and the ideas that inspire them!
TED talks often come across as just a little too pat, leaving the listener more with an illusion of understanding than a desire to ask more questions.
She makes an emphatic and compelling case that anything less than double-blind is irresponsible.
Like most (if not all) of us, the direction of my life has been significantly changed by meeting or hearing the right person at the right moment.
posts.key_insight: The best technical conferences are built around shared values and genuine enthusiasm rather than product promotion or company loyalty, creating space for authentic connection and serendipitous career-changing encounters.

posts.summary: Oracle's sudden mass layoff of approximately 90% of Solaris engineering marks the effective death of the proprietary operating system, with employees learning of their termination via pre-recorded calls in what Cantrill calls a 'depraved act of corporate violence.' The post contrasts the fragility of proprietary software—vulnerable to corporate whims and capable of dying instantly—with the resilience of open source, which 'cannot, even, die.' Cantrill argues that Solaris achieved immortality through its open source period, with illumos and technologies like ZFS and DTrace continuing to thrive despite Oracle's attempts to kill the community.
key_points: Oracle laid off ~90% of Solaris engineering, effectively killing the OS
Employees were notified via pre-recorded calls ('robo-RIF'd')
Proprietary software is inherently fragile and vulnerable to corporate decisions
Open source software is immortal—it can be neglected but never truly dies
illumos community survived Oracle's re-proprietarization and continues to thrive
Solaris's true life was its open source period, and its technologies live on
money_quotes: It is easier to destroy than to create.
In the long run – to paraphrase Keynes – proprietary software projects are all dead.
In stark contrast to proprietary software, open source does not – cannot, even – die.
Open source communities are robust because they are often united not only by circumstance, but by values.
Solaris may not have been truly born until it was made open source, and – certainly to me, anyway – it died the moment it was again made proprietary.
Born out of wedlock between Sun and AT&T and dying at the hands of a remorseless corporate sociopath a quarter century later.
posts.key_insight: While proprietary software can be killed instantly by corporate fiat, open source software achieves a form of immortality—surviving through community values and living on as both working code and technical literature for future generations.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill has compiled an extensive archive of his technical talks and recorded conversations spanning nearly two decades, organizing them into thematic trilogies covering debugging, open source, containers, DTrace, and software values. He specifically highlights 'Principles of Technology Leadership' and 'Coming of Age' as his most personally meaningful talks that represent his truest self. The post serves as both a practical index for those wanting to explore his work and a reflection on the themes that have defined his career in systems software.
key_points: Organized talks into thematic trilogies: Software Values, Debugging, Beloved (Papers We Love), Open Source, Containers, and DTrace
Two essential talks recommended above all others: 'Principles of Technology Leadership' and 'Coming of Age'
Career spans from proprietary software at Sun to open source advocacy at Joyent to hardware/software co-design at Oxide
Created Systems We Love conference inspired by Papers We Love, with emphatically double-blind selection process
Oxide and Friends podcast evolved from Twitter Spaces to become a vector for engineering wisdom and community
money_quotes: This potentially self-destructive behavior seems likely to have unwanted side-effects like spontaneous righteous indignation, superfluous historical metaphor, and near-lethal exposure to tangential anecdote – and yet I find myself compelled to enable it by collecting my erstwhile scattered talks.
These are the only talks that I have asked family and friends to watch, as they represent my truest self – or what I aspire that self to be, anyway.
In late 2016 and through 2017, it felt like fundamental values like decency and integrity were under attack; it seems appropriate that these three talks were born during this turbulent time.
While my career started developing proprietary software, I am blessed that most of it has been spent in open source.

In hindsight, the moment that I flipped the double bird was the moment that I forever lost moral authority with my children, who somehow managed to find that clip online and have never let me live it down.
posts.key_insight: A technologist's body of work, when curated and organized thematically, reveals the through-lines of values and passions that define not just a career but an identity.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill chronicles his journey from skeptical observer to enthusiastic adopter of Rust, explaining how the language won him over after initially dismissing it due to the M:N threading model. He praises Rust's willingness to admit and correct mistakes, its elegant error handling, powerful macro system, excellent performance, and welcoming community. Cantrill sees Rust as the language for the back half of his career—one that shares his values of permanence, robustness, and performance without forcing tradeoffs between them.
key_points: Rust's decision to remove M:N threading and segmented stacks showed rare courage to admit and fix mistakes
The propagation operator and Result type provide elegant, robust error handling without exceptions
Naive Rust code outperformed carefully optimized C by 32% due to better cache behavior
Rust's community succeeds by being inclusive in membership while resolute in shared values
The language enables 'nice things' through innovations that don't force tradeoffs between elegance and performance
money_quotes: All systems make mistakes, but few muster the courage to rectify them; on that basis alone, Rust became a project worthy of close attention.
One of our deepest, most fundamental problems as a craft is that we have enshrined 'velocity' above all else, willfully blinding ourselves to the long-term consequences of gimcrack software.
Among current languages, only Rust seems to share this aspiration for permanence, with a perspective that is decidedly larger than itself.
My naive Rust was ~32% faster than my carefully implemented C.

Instead of seeing Rust as a language to augment C and replace dynamic languages, I'm looking at it as a language to replace both C and dynamic languages in all but the very lowest layers of the stack.
In the open source era, community is critical: one is selecting a community as much as one is selecting a technology, as each informs the future of the other.
posts.key_insight: Rust represents a rare achievement in systems programming: a language that refuses to accept the traditional tradeoffs between safety, performance, and expressiveness, backed by a community with the courage to admit mistakes and the discipline to fix them.

posts.summary: Cantrill investigates why his Rust implementation of statemap outperformed his C implementation, diving deep into CPU performance counters and data structure analysis. He discovers that Rust's BTreeSet (B-tree) is more cache-efficient than C's AVL tree for modern memory hierarchies, and explores performance anomalies caused by HashMap load factors. The post demonstrates rigorous systems benchmarking methodology while concluding that Rust's design philosophy of 'powerful abstractions without sacrificing performance' enables optimized data structures that would be impractical to implement in C.
key_points: Rust version executed similar instruction count but with 28% fewer CPU cycles due to better cache behavior (96.9% vs 77.9% L1 hit rate)
Rust's BTreeSet is designed for modern memory hierarchies where cache misses are expensive, making B-trees sensible for in-memory use
Performance spikes in Rust were traced to HashMap load factor causing probing overhead near resize thresholds
C's intrusive data structures sidestep allocation/locking but make cache-optimized structures like B-trees impractical to implement
Rust's ownership model encourages orthogonal data structures that can be broadly used and carefully optimized
money_quotes: System benchmarking is one of those areas where – in Jonathan Swift's words from three centuries ago – 'falsehood flies, and the truth comes limping after it.'

I think that it is a reasonable assumption that, for any task, a lower-level language can always be made to outperform a higher-level one. But with that said, a pesky fact remains: I reimplemented a body of C software in Rust, and it performed better for the same task.
This is definitely a domain where an ounce of data is worth much more than a pound of hypotheses!
Rust really, really wants you to have complete orthogonality of purpose in your software.
All of this adds up to the existential win of Rust: powerful abstractions without sacrificing performance.
Does this mean that Rust will always outperform C? No, of course not. But it does mean that you shouldn't be surprised when it does.
posts.key_insight: Rust's design philosophy enforces separation of concerns and clear ownership trees, enabling highly optimized standard library data structures that would be impractical to implement as intrusive data structures in C.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that oversubscribed practitioner conferences like KubeCon should adopt double-blind review processes to ensure talks are selected on merit rather than the presenter's reputation or employer. Drawing from his experience organizing Systems We Love, he proposes a hybrid model: double-blind review until submissions are reduced to high-quality candidates, then de-blinding to shape the final program and amplify underrepresented voices. This approach led to surprising results at Systems We Love, where first-time speakers were accepted while 'big names' with weak submissions were rejected.
key_points: Double-blind review ensures content quality is based on submission merit, not presenter reputation or employer
A hybrid approach works well: blind review to filter quality, then de-blind to shape final program
First-time speakers often have excellent submissions that would be overlooked without blind review
Even 'big names' submit weak proposals - not every swing is a home run
13% acceptance rate means many quality submissions will be rejected regardless of process
money_quotes: Any oversubscribed conference needs to be double-blind to a very significant degree.
De-blinding at this point assures that the quality of the content is primarily due to the merit of the submission – not due to the particulars of the submitter.
Several 'big names' had submissions that we rejected while blinded – but looking at their submissions, the submissions themselves just weren't that great!
At least a quarter of the speakers were presenting for the first time.
I have been troubled by the strangulation of practitioner conferences by academic computer science.
posts.key_insight: Double-blind review is essential for oversubscribed conferences to ensure merit-based selection, but a hybrid approach that de-blinds for final program curation can help surface underrepresented voices while maintaining quality standards.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that assessing software engineering candidates requires evaluating five key traits—aptitude, education, motivation, values, and integrity—with heavy emphasis on written work samples rather than traditional whiteboard interviews. He criticizes the industry's over-reliance on algorithm quizzes and oral exams, advocating instead for code samples, writing samples, and analysis samples that show how engineers actually work. The post provides a detailed framework for the entire hiring process, from phone screens through post-interview deliberations, with particular attention to surfacing concerns from all interviewers regardless of seniority.
key_points: Traditional technical interviews focusing on algorithms are misguided because software engineering 'is not a spelling bee'
Candidates should submit three types of work samples: code, writing, and analysis
Writing good code and writing good prose are correlated skills in the best engineers
Values assessments should probe happiness and satisfaction rather than asking directly about values
Integrity checks are essential because bad actors can do 'nearly unbounded damage' to trust-based organizations
Post-interview meetings should start with 'Do not hire' positions to ensure reservations aren't silenced
money_quotes: Software engineering is not, in fact, a spelling bee, and one's ability to perform during an arbitrary oral exam may or may not correlate to one's ability to actually develop production software.

They must be able to build castles of imagination, and yet still understand the constraints of a grimy reality: they must be arrogant enough to see the world as it isn't, but humble enough to accept the world as it is.
Writing good code and writing good prose seem to be found together in the most capable software engineers.
Dishonest, manipulative people are, after all, dishonest and manipulative; they can easily fool people – and even references – into thinking that they are something that they are not.

Hiring processes can induce a kind of ultimate survivorship bias in that it is only those who have survived (or instituted) the process who are present to assess it – which can lead to deafening echo chambers of smug certitude.
If one is not motivated enough to learn about a potential employer, it's hard to see how they will suddenly gain the motivation to see them through difficult problems.
posts.key_insight: Effective software engineering hiring should prioritize written work samples and thoughtful assessment of values and integrity over traditional whiteboard interviews, because how candidates work independently reveals far more than how they perform under artificial oral examination.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that open source is experiencing a midlife crisis as companies try to restrict how their software can be used to compete with cloud providers, while still claiming open source benefits. He contends these restrictive licenses like Commons Clause and 'Community Licenses' are legally dubious, won't solve the underlying business model problem, and will only poison communities. The solution isn't licensing tricks but accepting that open source isn't a business model itself—companies must find complementary revenue through support, services, or building software they need and open-sourcing it for community benefits.
key_points: Open source companies want both open source benefits (community, adoption) and proprietary software benefits (lock-in, monopolistic rents)
Restrictive licenses like Commons Clause are hijacking open source nomenclature while asserting rights copyright holders don't actually have
Cloud providers will either use alternative open source implementations or reimplement proprietary bits themselves—they won't license proprietary software
Vague use-restriction licenses are 'corporate toxin' that poison communities during due diligence
Open source is like remote-friendly work policy: economically sensible but not a business model itself
Community health and longevity should be the focus, not licensing tricks that shackle communities to company fates
money_quotes: These companies want it both ways: they want the advantages of open source – the community, the positivity, the energy, the adoption, the downloads – but they also want to enjoy the fruits of proprietary software companies in software lock-in and its concomitant monopolistic rents.
Cloud services providers are emphatically not going to license your proprietary software.
Licenses that are vague with respect to permitted use are corporate toxin.
Open source software companies need to come to grips with that uncomfortable truth: their business model isn't their community's problem, and they should please stop trying to make it one.

Open source is like a remote-friendly working policy: it's something that you do because it makes economic and social sense; even as it's very core to your business, it's not a business model in and of itself.
By focusing on longevity, we can plainly see restrictive licensing as the death warrant that it is, shackling the fate of a community to that of a company.
posts.key_insight: Open source companies pursuing restrictive licensing to compete with cloud providers are making a midlife-crisis mistake—they should accept that open source isn't a business model and focus on community health rather than poisoning it with legally dubious restrictions.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill raises serious concerns about the Confluent Community License, arguing that it may actually be a EULA (End User License Agreement) masquerading as an open source license. After Jay Kreps of Confluent defended the license by comparing software to digital books, Cantrill points out that this comparison is troubling because both digital books and proprietary software use EULAs to claim users don't actually own their copies. He dissects the license's language showing it contains classic EULA boilerplate about not owning your copy while simultaneously granting modification rights, creating a contradictory 'frankenlicense' that tries to be both open and proprietary.
key_points: The Confluent Community License contains EULA-style boilerplate stating users don't own their copies of the software
EULAs are attempts to escape copyright law limitations and enter contract law where fewer restrictions exist
The license creates a contradiction by allowing modifications while claiming Confluent retains all rights to the software
Traditional open source licenses include crucial words like 'perpetual' and 'irrevocable' that are missing from this license
GitHub allowing EULAs in public repositories where git clone implies contract acceptance is legally problematic
money_quotes: The software is licensed, not sold.

We seem to be in some terrible new era of frankenlicenses, where the worst of proprietary licenses are bolted on to the goodwill created by open source licenses; we need your legal voices before these creatures destroy the village!
How can a license explicitly allow me to modify the software and at the same time tell me that I don't own the copy that I just modified?!
To say that a git clone is an implicit acceptance of a contract that happens to be sitting somewhere in the repository beggars belief.

EULAs are an attempt to get out of copyright law – where the copyright owner is quite limited in the rights afforded to them as to how the content is consumed – and into contract law, where there are many fewer such limits.
What I object to is trying to be at once open and proprietary; you must pick one.
posts.key_insight: Source-available licenses that claim users don't own their copies while granting modification rights are fundamentally contradictory 'frankenlicenses' that combine the worst aspects of proprietary EULAs with the appearance of open source.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on re-reading Tracy Kidder's 'The Soul of a New Machine' decades after it first inspired him as an undergraduate. He discovers that passages about organizational politics, leadership, and team dynamics that meant little to him as a student now resonate deeply with his career experiences. The book's exploration of why engineers build things—and why they persist through difficulty—leads him to conclude that shared mission and mutual trust are what enable teams to accomplish extraordinary feats.
key_points: The Soul of a New Machine should be considered computing's literary foundation—our Odyssey
Re-reading reveals new meaning: organizational rivalries and leadership complexities now resonate with career experience
Trust is risk, and risk-averse organizations struggle to foster mutual trust despite its obvious advantages
Engineers persist through difficulty not for ego or money, but because they are bonded by shared mission
The soul of what we build is shared—we do not endeavor alone but with like-minded individuals
money_quotes: I – like so many before and after me – found the book to be life changing: by telling the stories of the people behind the machine, the book showed the creative passion among engineers that might otherwise appear anodyne.

In any engineering career of sufficient length, a beloved project will at some point be shelved or killed – and the moment will be sufficiently traumatic to be seared into collective memory and memorialized in local lore.
West is right that trust is risk – and risk-averse organizations can really struggle to foster mutual trust, despite its obvious advantages.

When the problem is challenging and success uncertain, the endurance of engineers cannot be explained by these factors alone; regardless of why they start, engineers persist because they are bonded by a shared mission.
On teams that have a shared mission, mutual trust and ample grit, incredible things become possible.
The Soul of a New Machine serves to remind us that the soul of what we build is, above all, shared – that we do not endeavor alone but rather with a group of like-minded individuals.
posts.key_insight: The endurance of engineering teams through difficult challenges stems not from individual motivation but from the bonds of shared mission and mutual trust.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on his nine-year journey at Joyent, which far exceeded his original seven-year career planning horizon despite the typically short lifespan of venture-funded startups. He describes how Joyent's idiosyncratic culture and forward-thinking team helped them spot trends like Node.js and containers early, though being ahead of the market proved both blessing and curse. The post announces his departure and celebrates the company's wild ride through a metaphor of a musical with an exhaustive cast of characters.
key_points: Startups operate on accelerated timescales where seven years feels like seventy for established companies
Being ahead of the market is as much curse as blessing
Joyent's story could only be told as a musical due to its dramatic twists
The 'ex-Joyeur' game became a tradition to honor those who shared the journey
money_quotes: Seven years into the future for a startup is like seventy for a more established company.
Being ahead of the market is as much of a curse as a blessing, and Joyent lived many different lives over the past nine years.
The story of Joyent could only be told as a musical – an observation so profoundly true that any Joyeur will chuckle to themselves as they are reminded of it.
After nine years, I will go from a Joyeur to an ex-Joyeur – from a player to a piece on the board.
As a technologist, I am finding myself as excited as ever by snow-capped mountains on a distant horizon.
posts.key_insight: The startup journey is a theatrical experience where survival itself is an achievement, and the people who share that journey—whether they stay or go—become part of a shared story worth celebrating.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill announces the founding of Oxide Computer Company with Steve Tuck and Jess Frazelle, driven by his deep conviction that hardware and software must be built together. Drawing on frustrations from running cloud infrastructure on PC-era systems and watching hyperscalers keep their superior rack-level designs to themselves, he sees an opportunity to bring integrated, hyperscaler-class infrastructure to the broader market. The company aims to serve organizations that want cloud-like experiences on their own equipment, backed by Eclipse Ventures and legendary investor Pierre Lamond.
key_points: Hardware and software should be built with each other in mind - a belief Cantrill has held since joining Sun Microsystems in the mid-1990s
Running cloud infrastructure on PC-era systems causes deep technical pain, especially from firmware operating at cross purposes with systems software
Hyperscalers have developed superior rack-level designs but keep them cloistered, while Open Compute Project hardware lacks the software to realize its potential
As SaaS companies mature from growth-focused to margin-focused, more will consider owning infrastructure rather than renting
Shared values among co-founders are paramount - more important than shared technical vision
money_quotes: Starting a computer company may sound crazy (and you would certainly be forgiven a double-take!), but it stems from a belief that I hold in my marrow: that hardware and software should each be built with the other in mind.
I was haunted by the pain and futility of trying to build a cloud with PC-era systems.
Their rack-level designs are a clear win – why are these designs cloistered among so few?
Even as the world is moving (or has moved) to elastic, API-driven computing, there remain good reasons to run on one's own equipment!

Most important of all (with the emphasis itself being a reflection of hard-won wisdom), we three share deeply-held values: we have the same principled approach, with shared aspirations for building the kind of company that customers will love to buy from – and employees will be proud to work for.
posts.key_insight: The gap between hyperscaler-class infrastructure and what's available to everyone else represents both a technical failure and a market opportunity that can only be solved by a company willing to integrate hardware and software from the ground up.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill writes a memorial tribute to Khaled Bichara, an angel investor in Oxide Computer who died in a car accident in Cairo. He remembers Khaled as both a bold investor with appreciation for hard technical problems and a profoundly decent person dedicated to his family, companies, and country. The post reflects on Khaled's work fostering entrepreneurship in Egypt and expresses the team's grief at losing someone they had hoped to journey with for years.
key_points: Khaled Bichara was an Oxide angel investor who died in a car accident in Cairo
He was known for appreciating hard technical problems while being a deeply caring person
His work kindling entrepreneurship in Egypt inspired many
Oxide was looking forward to a long partnership with him
money_quotes: Those of us who have known Khaled for years have known him to be a bold investor who appreciated hard technical problems â and also a profoundly decent person, who cared deeply for his family, his companies, and his country.
Khaledâs stories of kindling entrepreneurship in Egypt were inspiring to any who heard them â and served as a reminder of the reponsibility that we all have to our broader communities.
For our part, we were looking forward to a long journey together, and to making good on his belief in us; we are gutted to have lost him so abruptly.
Silicon Valley may be a long way from Cairo, but his impact and legacy will be felt here for years to come.
posts.key_insight: The best investors combine bold technical vision with profound human decency, and their impact extends far beyond the companies they fund to the communities and entrepreneurs they inspire.

posts.summary: Cantrill urges readers to immediately watch Ava DuVernay's documentary '13th' about systemic racism in America's criminal justice system. Writing during the 2020 George Floyd protests, he argues that white Americans habitually avoid difficult subjects and that the current moment demands action. He challenges readers that if they won't spend 100 minutes on this documentary now, they should stop performative activism like posting black squares. The post combines a personal recommendation from his co-founder Steve Tuck with a broader call to watch, share, and support criminal justice reform.
key_points: White Americans habitually avoid difficult subjects in their viewing habits
13th reveals connections between mass incarceration, racism, and the prison-industrial complex
The documentary features a surprisingly diverse range of voices including Angela Davis and Newt Gingrich
Performative activism without substantive engagement is meaningless
Watching and understanding is just the first step; systemic rethinking is required
money_quotes: We – we all, but especially white Americans – run away from difficult subjects.
If you are unwilling to watch this now, be honest with yourself and realize that you are never going to watch it.
If you do not have 100 minutes to give to this most important topic at this most critical moment, you are not actually willing to do anything at all.
Please spare us all the black squares and the trending hashtags.
We need to not merely reform this system, we need to rethink it entirely.
posts.key_insight: Performative solidarity without substantive personal engagement—like refusing to spend 100 minutes watching a documentary during a crisis about that very issue—reveals that one isn't actually willing to do anything at all.

posts.summary: Two years after initially falling in love with Rust, Cantrill reports that the relationship has deepened rather than frayed. At Oxide Computer Company, Rust has proven remarkably well-suited across the entire stack from firmware to REST APIs. He catalogs fourteen specific features he appreciates—from no_std's embedded support to data-bearing enums to the anyhow! crate—while emphasizing that Rust's underlying values, not just its surface features, make it a lasting technical relationship.
key_points: Rust's no_std feature enables safe, powerful embedded development with binaries as small as C
Data-bearing enums and algebraic types fundamentally improve protocol parsing and error handling
The Rust ecosystem (Gimli, Goblin, anyhow!, paste!) reflects the quality of developers attracted to the language
Rust's expression-oriented design provides ternary operator power without the pitfalls
The language's early pruning of redundant syntax prevented it from becoming baroque and write-only
Working in Rust reveals how numb C programmers become to constant unsafety
money_quotes: The nothing that C provides reflects history more than minimalism; it is not an elegant nothing, but rather an ill-considered nothing that leaves those who build embedded systems building effectively everything themselves – and in a language that does little to help them write correct software.
To implement in Rust is to eat the fruit from the tree of knowledge of unsafe programs – and to go back to unsafe code is to realize that you were naked all along!

This pruning in Rust's early days – the idea that syntax should carry its weight by bringing unique expressive power – has kept Rust from the fate of languages that suffered from debilitating addictions to new syntax and concomitant complexity overdose.
When there is more than one way to do it for absolutely everything, a language becomes so baroque as to become write-only!

When getting into a long-term relationship with anything – be it a person, or a company, or a technology – it can be tempting to look at its surface characteristics... we sometimes look too much at what things are rather than what guides them.
posts.key_insight: A technology relationship's longevity depends not on surface features but on alignment of underlying values—and Rust's values around safety, minimalism, and empowering systems programmers make it a generational fit for serious infrastructure work.

posts.summary: Cantrill explains Oxide Computer's radical approach to compensation: paying every employee the exact same salary ($175,000, later raised). He argues this uniform, transparent model embodies the company's values of honesty, teamwork, and empathy while eliminating the toxic dynamics of traditional compensation structures. The post methodically addresses objections about scalability, geographic pay differences, and career ladders, contending that paying for work rather than circumstances is both more ethical and more conducive to collaboration.
key_points: Oxide pays all 23 employees identical salaries regardless of role, experience, or location
Traditional compensation structures breed dishonesty and perverse individual incentives that harm teamwork
Geographic pay differences are just companies paying less 'because they can', not genuine cost-of-living adjustments
Transparent uniform pay eliminates salary negotiation anxiety and enables candid feedback
The model may not scale forever, but the underlying values of transparency and fairness will endure
money_quotes: Compensation isn't merely about the currency we redeem in exchange for our labors, but rather it is a proxy for how we are valued in a larger organization.
The need to 'quantify' one's performance in exchange for justifying changes to individual compensation are at the root of much of what's wrong in the tech industry.

Companies spin this by explaining they are merely paying people based on their cost of living, but this is absurd: do we increase someone's salary when their spouse loses their job or when their kid goes to college?
The truth is that companies pay people less in other geographies for a simple reason: because they can.

By eliminating the rung of compensation, we can put the focus on career development where it belongs: on supporting one another in our self-improvement, and working together to do things that are beyond any one of us.
It's also true that startup equity doesn't pay the orthodontist's bill or get the basement repainted.
posts.key_insight: Compensation structures reveal a company's true values, and uniform transparent pay eliminates the individual competition that poisons teamwork while forcing honest conversations about what work is actually worth.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill reflects on the rise of social audio platforms, comparing Twitter Spaces favorably to Clubhouse while drawing parallels to 1980s talk radio. He argues that Clubhouse's iOS-only approach and exclusionary design fundamentally contradict the promise of democratized conversation, while Twitter Spaces offers a more inclusive alternative that combines the best aspects of podcasts (multitasking) with genuine interactivity. His early experiences hosting and participating in Spaces have been overwhelmingly positive, marked by playful discovery, meaningful cross-cultural conversations, and a civility often absent from written online discourse.
key_points: Twitter Spaces combines podcast-style multitasking with real-time interactivity, filling a gap left by talk radio's decline
Clubhouse's iOS-only strategy and celebrity focus reveal fundamental elitism that undermines social audio's democratic potential
Voice conversations naturally encourage empathy and civility in ways written online discourse cannot
The medium enables unexpected, meaningful connections across geographic and social boundaries
Social audio represents a genuinely new format that hasn't existed since the decline of call-in radio
money_quotes: To me, the deprioritization of Android reflects Clubhouse's deeper problem: it is fundamentally elitist and exclusionary.
It says it right there on the tin: it is, at the end of the day, a clubhouse – not a cafe or a town square.

I personally have no interest in participating in venues that deliberately limit the participants: I want to engage with the broadest possible cross-section, not some subset that has been manicured by circumstance or technology choice.
One of the challenges of the written word is that it's too easy to be nasty: our mirror neurons don't fire when our damage is inflicted at a distance.
It is really nice to have social networking time after which one looks back at a clean kitchen or a prepared meal!

Clubhouse feels like Friendster. And just as Friendster hit on something huge (social networking!) for the wrong reasons (the founder's desire to find dates!), Clubhouse's focus on listening to famous people feels misplaced.
posts.key_insight: Social audio succeeds when it prioritizes broad accessibility and genuine conversation over exclusivity and celebrity worship, making Twitter Spaces' inclusive approach a fundamental advantage over Clubhouse's elitist design.

posts.summary: Oxide Computer developed Hubris, a new embedded operating system written entirely in Rust, after finding that existing open source operating systems didn't meet their security and robustness requirements. The system's key innovation is specifying all tasks at build time rather than allowing dynamic task creation at runtime, eliminating entire classes of failure modes related to resource exhaustion. Cantrill also developed Humility, a companion debugger, and both projects are now open source as part of Oxide's belief that open source is overdue in firmware and low-level systems software.
key_points: Existing embedded operating systems didn't meet Oxide's security and robustness requirements
Hubris specifies all tasks at build time, eliminating dynamic resource exhaustion failures
The system is microkernel-based with memory protection, even though it's written in memory-safe Rust
Cantrill developed Humility debugger to complement Hubris
Both projects are now open source, reflecting Oxide's belief in open source firmware
money_quotes: Dynamic resource exhaustion is the root of many problems in embedded systems; having the system know a priori all of the tasks that it will ever see liberates it from not just a major source of dynamic allocation, but also from the concomitant failure modes.
It is hard to have a fork bomb when the system lacks fork itself!
In this regard, Hubris represents what we like about Rust, too: a creative solution that cuts through a false dichotomy to yield a system that is at once nimble and rigorous.
We also believe that the open source revolution — and its many advantages for customers — are long overdue in the lowest layers of the software stack.
And yes, even in memory safe languages like Rust, memory protection is essential!
posts.key_insight: By rejecting the conventional wisdom that operating systems must support dynamic task creation, Hubris achieves both the flexibility of general-purpose systems and the reliability of fully static embedded systems.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on his complicated history with Twitter—from debugging its early failures to using it as Oxide's primary marketing channel—as Elon Musk's chaotic takeover threatens to destroy the platform. He argues that Musk fundamentally misunderstands the business (advertisers are the customers, not users) and is making catastrophic mistakes by threatening departing advertisers and bungling layoffs. Drawing a parallel to the fall of the Berlin Wall, Cantrill predicts Twitter's collapse will usher in a fragmented social media landscape where no single platform dominates, and he's moving to Mastodon as a more civic, earnest alternative.
key_points: Musk doesn't understand Twitter's business model—advertisers are the customers, and they can leave immediately
Threatening customers who reduce spending is executive incompetence of the highest order
Twitter as a unified social platform will become a relic; the future is multiple specialized networks
Mastodon offers a more relaxed, civic experience without the engagement-maximizing toxicity
When change finally arrives after long stability, it happens faster than anyone thought possible
money_quotes: Musk has absolutely no idea what he's doing, having screwed up the most basic element of the business: he doesn't even know who the customer is!
Does it need to be said that menacing customers who choose to buy less from you doesn't really work in a free economy?
Yes, the status quo can maintain itself for a very, very long time – but when change finally arrives, it can happen faster than anyone would have thought possible.
Twitter as an idea – a single social platform catering to all demographics and uses – will become like the evening nightly news or the morning newspaper: a relic from a bygone era.
And as if this fecal pie were wanting for a topping, there is a fetid cherry on top: deep, bungled layoffs.
It will probably be kicked around, bought and sold a few times, before it finally lands as a SlideShare-like ghost town, where you need to watch a 15 second ad before every dril tweet.
posts.key_insight: Musk's incompetent takeover of Twitter will accelerate the inevitable fragmentation of social media into specialized platforms, ending the era of one network serving all demographics.

posts.summary: Cantrill draws a compelling parallel between the current migration to decentralized social networks like Mastodon and the homebrew computing movement of the late 1970s. He argues that centralized social networks have passed their 'Disrupt By' date, with advertising-based models creating perverse incentives that promote divisiveness and rage engagement. The shift to decentralized platforms, while potentially messy and uncomfortable, represents a return to genuine social connection free from algorithmic manipulation, where users must actively choose who to follow rather than being fed content designed to maximize engagement.
key_points: Computing history oscillates between centralization and decentralization, each with distinct tradeoffs
Advertising-based social networks algorithmically promote rage because engagement correlates with outrage
Mastodon's lack of algorithms means content only comes from people you actively follow
The follower/following ratio obsession on Twitter led people to become isolated and fed purely by algorithms
Decentralized networks force users to actively listen by following others, potentially fostering less divisiveness
money_quotes: One of the most persistent cycles in the history of computing is the oscillation between centralization and decentralization.
The progression here now seems painfully clear in hindsight: advertising-based models demand engagement – and algorithms will naturally observe that when we are enraged, we stay engaged.

The net effect of all of this is that while we may find this engaging, we certainly don't find it to be enjoyable: I (like many, I suspect) have resented the attention of mine that it has held – like an extraordinarily bad reality TV program that I can't stop myself from bingewatching.

It is opting to be fed exclusively by the algorithm, losing any tether to genuine social connection. It is perhaps unsurprising that the people that I saw do this rapidly descended into dark, bizarre thinking.
If you want to receive a letter, you need to send a letter!
posts.key_insight: Decentralized social networking, like the homebrew computing movement before it, represents a necessary correction where the messiness of user control is vastly preferable to the algorithmic manipulation of centralized platforms that profit from division.

posts.summary: Oxide Computer announces general availability of the world's first commercial cloud computer alongside a $44M Series A. Cantrill argues that while cloud computing's dominance is uncontroversial, the ability to purchase (not just rent) cloud infrastructure is increasingly recognized as necessary, and building such a computer requires rack-scale co-design of hardware and software. The post highlights how Oxide's integrated approach—from whisper-quiet operation to blindmated networking to fully included open source software—represents a fundamental departure from the traditional 'kit car' approach of assembling disparate components.
key_points: Cloud computers should be purchasable, not just rentable—a view gaining mainstream acceptance
Rack-level co-design of hardware and software is essential but requires extraordinary cross-disciplinary teams
Oxide shipped a complete rack in one box, reducing deployment from weeks to hours
The rack is whisper quiet even at 15kW due to efficient 80mm fan design
All networking is blindmated—no cables needed to add compute capacity
All software is open source with no separate licensing
money_quotes: People who are really serious about software should make their own hardware.
The only surprise is how surprising it continues to be for some folks.
We opted for the steeper path in the name of saving our customers grief.
We believe that cloud computers should come complete with all system software built-in, and with no additional licensing to manage or to pay for.
You don't need to worry about hinky open core models or relicensing surprises.
posts.key_insight: Building a true cloud computer requires the courage to vertically integrate hardware and software at rack scale—a path that's harder but ultimately delivers the seamless experience customers actually need.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that the tech industry has allowed software vendors to capture the economic benefits of hardware improvements through per-core licensing, a practice that has become especially egregious as CPU advances now manifest primarily in core count rather than clock speed. He traces the shift from Amazon's customer-friendly price cuts to margin-maximizing depreciation extensions, and criticizes the PC-era separation of hardware and software that enables enterprise vendors to tax infrastructure improvements. Oxide's approach of including all software with hardware purchases is positioned as returning Moore's Law dividends to their rightful owners: the users of compute.
key_points: Cloud providers have shifted from passing hardware improvements to customers via price cuts to capturing them through extended depreciation
Per-core software licensing allows third parties to tax infrastructure improvements they neither developed nor paid for
The hardware/software divide in enterprise computing creates finger-pointing and prevents beneficial co-design
Software costs can absorb entire gains from next-generation CPUs, creating disincentives to upgrade infrastructure
Oxide includes all software with hardware purchases, ensuring hardware improvements benefit users
money_quotes: Per-core licensing allows a third party—who neither made the significant investment in developing the next generation of microprocessor nor paid for the part themselves—to exact a tax on improved infrastructure.
Software costs can potentially absorb the entire gain from a next-generation CPU, leaving a disincentive to run newer, more efficient infrastructure.

In the go-go era of Dennard scaling (when clock rates rose at a blistering rate), software vendors never would have been allowed to get away with charging by the gigahertz; we should not allow them to feel so emboldened to charge by core count now!
This gives the dividends of Moore's Law to whom they rightfully belong: the users of compute.
And yes, it's all open source—which unfortunately demands the immediate clarification that it's actually open source rather than pretend open source.
posts.key_insight: Per-core software licensing is an industry-accepted practice that unjustly allows software vendors to capture the economic benefits of hardware innovation that rightfully belong to the users who purchased that hardware.

posts.summary: Cantrill uses a recent bug-fixing incident at Oxide to illustrate how their deliberately cultivated engineering culture works in practice. He highlights Demo Friday as a key cultural institution that emerged organically and now serves as a venue for peer recognition and cross-domain learning. The post argues that Oxide's culture—characterized by openness, curiosity, and non-hierarchical collaboration—is the result of intentional design starting with mission, principles, and values that are reinforced through hiring. While acknowledging the ambiguity inherent in culture-building, Cantrill maintains that the hard work of creating deliberate-but-not-prescriptive norms pays off in how the team operates.
key_points: Demo Friday evolved from a single team's practice to a company-wide institution that drives peer recognition and cross-functional learning
Oxide's culture deliberately rewards openness and curiosity while discouraging defensiveness and empire-building
The company uses mission, principles, and values as the foundation for hiring, creating a self-reinforcing culture
Cultural implementation includes uniform compensation, writing-intensive communication, no formal performance reviews, and recorded meetings
Culture-building requires embracing ambiguity—it's 'gooey, squishy' rather than engineered like software
money_quotes: Demo Friday is important because it gets to one of our most important drivers as technologists: the esteem of our peers.
Our culture rewards openness, curiosity, and communication, and discourages defensiveness, empire-building, and gatekeeping.
The only drawback is that it's hard work: culture has to be deliberate without being overly prescriptive, and that can be a tricky balance.
Building a culture is very different than building software: it is not engineered in a traditional sense, but is rather a gooey, squishy, organism that will evolve over time.
We have often found that a demo that feels small to someone in the thick of work will feel extraordinary to someone outside of it.
posts.key_insight: Engineering culture requires the same deliberate attention as engineering products, but unlike software it must be cultivated as a living organism that balances intentionality with room to evolve.

posts.summary: Cantrill responds to Paul Graham's 'Founder Mode' essay by arguing that the key to maintaining company vision isn't mysterious founder intuition, but rather a writing-intensive culture that codifies values and builds trust. He contends that documenting decisions through processes like Oxide's RFDs allows founders to scale their thinking without micromanaging. The piece warns that founders may misuse 'founder mode' as license for seagull management, when the real goal should be building systems that foster mutual trust as the team grows.
key_points: Writing-intensive culture is key to scaling founder vision
Shared values must be documented and used as hiring criteria
Trust is the essential ingredient Graham's essay undervalues
Founders risk using 'founder mode' to justify micromanagement
money_quotes: There does seem to be a clear component for keeping companies true to themselves: the written word.
A writing- (and reading-!) intensive company culture does, in fact, allow for scaling the kind of responsibility that Graham thinks of as founder mode.
The most important quality in a leader is to bind a team with mutual trust: with it, all things are possible — and without it, even easy things can be debilitatingly difficult.

Founders are at grave risk of misinterpreting Graham's 'Founders Mode' to be a license to micromanage their teams, descending into the kind of manic seagull management that inhibits a team rather than empowering it.
Build the trust that allows a team to kick a Jobsian dent in the universe!
posts.key_insight: The antidote to losing founder vision isn't mysterious 'founder mode' but rather building a writing-intensive culture that codifies values and creates the mutual trust needed to scale responsibility across a growing team.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill and Steve Tuck pay tribute to Charles Beeler, a venture capitalist who passed away from cancer. Beeler was an early investor in both Joyent and Oxide Computer, distinguished by his hands-on approach during difficult times and his genuine enthusiasm for creators and innovators. The post highlights how Beeler persistently encouraged people to do their best work, including pushing Cantrill to deliver a candid keynote that became one of his most meaningful talks.
key_points: Charles Beeler was an unusual VC who served on the ACM Queue board, bringing a different perspective to practitioner content
Unlike investors who hide during hard times, Beeler actively worked to fix problems at Joyent, including leading the search for new executive leadership
Beeler's persistence led to Cantrill's 'Platform as Reflection of Values' talk, pushing him to speak candidly about the strained Joyent/node.js relationship
Beeler invested in Oxide despite it being a challenging fit for his fund, working to address objections and winning over his partnership
money_quotes: Charles is one of the good ones, you know.
When companies hit challenging times, some investors will hide—but Charles was the kind of investor to figure out how to fix what was broken.
If the Joyent relationship with node.js had become strained, so much more the reason to speak candidly about it!
He worked behind the scenes to encourage people to do their best work, always with an enthusiasm for the innovators and the creators.
You were the best of venture capital. Closer to the heart, you were a terrific friend to us both.
posts.key_insight: The best investors distinguish themselves not by picking winners but by rolling up their sleeves during hard times and persistently encouraging founders to do ambitious, honest work.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on twenty years of blogging, tracing his journey from Sun Microsystems' pioneering blogs.sun.com initiative through WordPress purgatory to finally hosting his blog on Oxide's own cloud computer. He recounts how Sun's explicit trust in employees to blog publicly created extraordinary technical content, and how blogging became essential for documenting the human stories behind projects like DTrace, ZFS, Manta, and Oxide. The piece culminates in advice for aspiring bloggers: write from the heart, because even if no one else reads it, your future self will thank you.
key_points: Sun's blogs.sun.com initiative in 2004 explicitly trusted and encouraged employees to blog, creating unprecedented technical content
The beauty of blogging is writing only when you have something to say, on whatever topic you want
Much valuable content was lost when Sun sank, highlighting the importance of owning your own writing
Static site generators represent the ideal approach: fast, clean, git-backed, free from captive content management
Two decades of blogging document the full human experience: births of projects and children, love of technologies, deaths of companies and colleagues
The throughline of enduring writing is that it reflects the humanity in technical endeavors
money_quotes: The message was clear, and it was explicit: 'We trust you.'
The beauty of blogging is that you needn't be regular and you also needn't be on topic: you can write only when you have something to say – and you can say whatever you want.

There was so much great content that the only downside – such as it was – was that it was shackled to a listing corporate vessel. And when that vessel sank, much of the content went to a watery grave.
This WordPress site had the curse of being just good enough.
The throughline of two decades of writing, especially for the pieces that have enduring resonance: they reflect the humanity in our endeavor.
Write from the heart, even if you think no one is reading it; if nothing else, your future self will thank you for it!
posts.key_insight: The most enduring technical writing reflects the humanity in our endeavor—write from the heart, and your future self will thank you for the authentic record of your journey.

posts.summary: Cantrill announces dtrace.conf(24), the latest in an Olympiad-cadence series of DTrace conferences that began in 2008. After skipping the pandemic years, they've decided to go fully online for the first time, recognizing that the rise of remote work has dispersed technologists and made travel-dependent conferences less practical. The post reflects on how tech conferences have evolved—with O'Reilly going online-only and free conferences like P99 CONF proving the model—while preserving some traditions like commemorative t-shirts and an optional in-person social event at the Oxide office.
key_points: dtrace.conf began in 2008 as a small unconference where everyone had to present
The conference follows an 'Olympiad cadence' every four years (2008, 2012, 2016, skipped 2020)
Going online-only reflects how remote work has dispersed the tech community
Chat-based hallway tracks can be more effective and scalable than physical hallways
The shared values that created the DTrace community continue to endure after 17 years
money_quotes: With talks all recorded and made publicly available after the conference, how does one justify attending a conference in person?
Using chat as the hallway track can be more much effective (and is certainly more scalable!) than the actual physical hallways at a conference.

Without the need to feed and shelter participants, the logistics for running a conference become much more tenable—and the price point can be lowered to the point that even highly produced conferences like P99 CONF can be made freely available.
If anything, it was a little too good: we realized that we couldn't recreate the magic, and we demurred on making it an annual event.
Much has changed since I sent that e-mail 17 years ago—but the shared values and disposition that brought together our small community continue to endure.
posts.key_insight: The shift to remote work has fundamentally changed the economics and logistics of technical conferences, making online-only formats not just viable but often superior for content-focused gatherings.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill argues that despite Pat Gelsinger's positive personal qualities, he was fundamentally wrong for Intel because he embodied and reinforced the company's core cultural problem: arrogance. Drawing from Oxide's experience as a Tofino customer, Cantrill traces Intel's pattern of killing innovative products, failing to listen to customers or employees, and maintaining a peacetime mentality (keeping dividends) during an existential crisis. The ultimate proof came when Intel killed Tofino despite assurances, betraying partners and demonstrating the very cultural dysfunction that needed fixing.
key_points: Gelsinger's oral history revealed concerning arrogance, including claiming NVIDIA would be 'a fourth the size' if he'd stayed at Intel to lead Larrabee
Intel's fundamental problem is cultural: inherited arrogance that prevents listening to employees or customers
Maintaining the dividend instead of eliminating it was Gelsinger's first major mistake - a peacetime act when Intel needed a wartime CEO
Intel Foundry Services struggles because the culture isn't amenable to customer engagement that foundry customers expect
Intel killed Tofino despite repeated assurances, proving they cannot be trusted as a partner
Oxide is moving to Xsight Labs' X2 ASIC, which offers both Tofino's strengths and potential for open programmable networking
money_quotes: Intel has a long, long (long!) track record of fostering innovation outside of its mainstay x86 product—and then killing it.
Intel needed a wartime CEO—but in maintaining a dividend that it couldn't afford, Gelsinger was committing the ultimate peacetime act.
A concrete manifestation of the company's arrogance is that it didn't listen: it didn't listen to its own people and it didn't listen to its customers.
How can Intel ever expected to be trusted when they treat partners this way?
As Gelsinger returned, I found no reassurance in the company's narrative, which felt less like the arrival of Winston Wolf, and more like a cherished prince returning home to live out his destiny.
Everyone we dealt with at Intel agreed that the move was short-sighted, but as with so many things Intel (that culture problem again!), they felt powerless to change it.
posts.key_insight: Intel's crisis required a leader who could dismantle decades of inherited arrogance and truly listen to employees and customers, but Gelsinger instead reinforced the company's worst cultural impulses about its own exceptionalism.

posts.summary: Four years after publicly explaining Oxide's uniform compensation model, Cantrill reflects on how it has worked in practice as the company grew from 23 to 75 employees. Rather than causing people to slack off as skeptics predicted, the model has driven employees to take their own performance and peer hiring extremely seriously. The uniform pay structure has enabled organizational flexibility, eliminated toxic middle management dynamics, liberated feedback from compensation politics, and fostered genuine teamwork built on mutual trust.
key_points: Uniform compensation has increased rather than decreased employee self-motivation and performance standards
The model forces rigorous peer-based hiring since everyone is compensated as equals
It eliminates the middle management burden of determining compensation differentials
Feedback becomes genuinely constructive when decoupled from compensation decisions
The approach enables role flexibility and cross-functional collaboration without career anxiety
money_quotes: When some outsiders hear about our compensation model, they insist that it can't possibly work because 'everyone will slack off.' I have come to find this concern to be more revealing of the person making the objection than of our model.
The specific dismissal of support roles reveals a widespread and corrosive devaluation of those closest to customers.
What fraction of middle management in a company is dedicated to—at some level—determining who gets what in terms of compensation? What happens when you eliminate that burden completely?
Performance review processes aren't, in fact, about improving the performance of the team, but rather quantifying and stack-ranking that performance for purposes of compensation.
A dirty open secret of Silicon Valley middle management: its ranks are taught to grade their career by the number of reports in their organization.
The currency of a team is mutual trust, and while uniform compensation certainly isn't the only way to achieve that trust, boy does it ever help!
posts.key_insight: Uniform compensation doesn't reduce motivation—it redirects energy from internal political competition toward genuine contribution and teamwork.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill reflects on the discontinuation of USENIX ATC, a conference he once lionized but watched decline over two decades as it became overly academic and disconnected from practitioners. He argues that while the conference failed, systems innovation itself thrived through open source—with Go, Rust, and production systems flourishing outside traditional academic publishing. The conference model of publishing, combined with the inherent difficulty of in-person events, ultimately doomed ATC, though Cantrill acknowledges it was once a singular forum for pioneering systems work.
key_points: USENIX ATC declined because it became too academic and lost connection to industry practitioners
Open source became the new vector for systems innovation, enabling languages like Go and Rust that couldn't arise in a proprietary world
The conference model of publishing in computer science was fundamentally flawed compared to journals
In-person conferences face inherent challenges that online formats address more cost-effectively
Despite its decline, ATC at its height was a singular forum for pioneering systems work
money_quotes: Go and Rust do not arise—cannot arise—in a proprietary world. These are also not the work of hobbyists: they were written by professionals for professionals in a professional capacity.
Importantly, the vector for publishing for the practitioner is via repositories rather than being confined to only formal writing.
So USENIX ATC did finally succumb, and I do view it as a casualty here, asphyxiated by the rough love of academic computer science and its inability to grow beyond the conference model of publishing.
While they certainly lose something in the process, it feels like online conferences give us indisputably (overwhelmingly!) more bang for the buck.
ATC was—at its height—a singular forum for presenting pioneering systems work.
posts.key_insight: Open source repositories have replaced academic conferences as the primary vector for systems innovation, enabling production-focused work that couldn't exist in a proprietary world while traditional conferences like USENIX ATC suffocated under academic publishing models.

posts.summary: Cantrill draws an extended analogy between the college baseball recruiting process and raising venture capital, arguing that both involve asymmetric bets on the future with high stakes, FOMO-driven decision-making, and similar dynamics around offers, negotiations, and optionality. Through his experience as both a startup founder and father of a college baseball player, he maps VC concepts like term sheets, preemptive offers, valuation overhangs, and down rounds onto the college athletics recruiting landscape. The piece serves as both a guide to navigating these parallel worlds and a critique of bad actors who exploit the power imbalance in both systems.
key_points: College athletics recruiting has become a parallel universe requiring years of dedication, fundamentally different from the non-athlete college experience
NIL money and the transfer portal have transformed college sports into something resembling a VC-funded startup ecosystem
Both VCs and coaches engage in 'long maybes' - preserving optionality while stringing along counterparties who would be better served by a clear no
Verbal commitments to high school underclassmen are entirely non-binding and represent a predatory 'preemption' practice
Going to a Power Four school too early is like raising at too high a valuation - it can create expectations impossible to meet
Athletes and entrepreneurs should go where they're truly wanted, recognizing that conviction and praise are not the same thing
money_quotes: VCs call this 'preserving optionality', but it is in fact a 'long maybe'—and it leads to long paths-to-nowhere with VCs that seemingly always want something else to prolong the conversation.
The athlete—like the entrepreneur—is trying to manage their own fate rather than a portfolio; they don't get to apply portfolio theory to the one career they get to live.
Be wary of where you are not really wanted, and note that conviction and praise are not the same thing: anyone wanting to get you over the line will tell you what you want to hear.
These coaches become infamous to players over their careers, and the athletes that they have wronged live for the opportunity to face these coaches and win.
The truth in both cases is that the institution just isn't that interested; both startup and athlete would be well-advised to pencil them in as a 'no' and move on.
posts.key_insight: Both venture capital fundraising and college athletic recruiting are asymmetric coupling processes where institutions bet on futures they can't predict, creating similar dynamics of FOMO, non-binding commitments, and power imbalances that both entrepreneurs and athletes must learn to navigate by seeking genuine conviction rather than mere interest.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill analyzes the increasingly dire financial and operational situation facing John Fisher's Athletics franchise as it stumbles through a disastrous Sacramento residency while failing to secure funding for a Las Vegas stadium. The 'groundbreaking' ceremony revealed itself as a sham with rented construction equipment, exposing that Fisher lacks the approximately $1 billion needed to complete the project. Cantrill systematically dismantles Fisher's three potential funding options—debt, equity, and liquidating personal assets—concluding that none are viable enough to save the Vegas stadium plan. He predicts this will culminate in MLB finally forcing Fisher to sell the team after the 2028 opening day deadline becomes impossible to meet.
key_points: The Athletics refused to put Sacramento's name on the team while plastering 'Visit Las Vegas' ads in the outfield, alienating the host city
Construction equipment at the 'groundbreaking' was rented as props because actual construction hasn't started due to lack of funding

Fisher needs approximately $1 billion but faces problems with all funding options: banks won't lend to a team with questionable cash flow projections, equity investors won't pay the $2B valuation Fisher wants, and liquidating his Gap stock and Earthquakes stake would trigger massive tax bills
The A's have had social media replies turned off since 2023—a devastating red flag for any consumer brand
Stadium costs have ballooned from $1.5B toward $2B due to steel tariffs and construction inflation
money_quotes: In this Potemkin groundbreaking, even the construction equipment was a sham.
It's hard to find the dumb money when you are, in fact, the dumb money.
It is hard to imagine a bigger red flag for a consumer-facing brand: a brand that has so thoroughly alienated so many customers that it lives in fear of customers entirely.

It would be gloriously fitting for John Fisher, whose two existential fears are clearly that he is an incapable dunce and that he will fumble his birthright, to manage to confirm both in a single, spectacular disaster.
In just a few months John Fisher has achieved in Sacramento what took him years in Oakland: fans that hate his guts.
The incompetence of this ownership knows no depth or bounds: they will always find new ways to screw up.
posts.key_insight: John Fisher's Athletics franchise is trapped in a financial death spiral where his refusal to sell control, combined with an alienated fanbase and ballooning stadium costs, makes all three funding paths—debt, equity, or personal liquidation—effectively impossible, inevitably leading to a forced sale.

posts.summary: Oxide Computer Company has raised a $100M Series B led by USIT, more than doubling their total capital raised to date. The post recounts their journey from skeptical VCs in 2019 who doubted the market existed, through building an integrated hardware-software stack from first principles, to shipping their first system and achieving growing customer adoption. The funding will enable them to scale manufacturing, systems, and operations to meet increasing enterprise demand.
key_points: Series B of $100M led by USIT more than doubles Oxide's total capital raised from $89M over six years
Early VCs had it backwards: they believed Oxide could build it but doubted the market, when actually the technical risk was enormous but the market was clearly there
Oxide built everything from scratch: board designs, microcontroller OS, platform enablement software, hypervisor, switch, storage service, and control plane
Customer sales cycles shortened dramatically due to Oxide's radical transparency via podcasts, RFDs, documentation, and open source code
The funding addresses customer questions about scale: manufacturing, system size, and operations at larger deployments
money_quotes: We know you can build this, but we don't think that there is a market.

We felt that there was in fact substantial technical risk in the enormity of the task we put before ourselves—but we also knew that if we could build it (a huge if!) there was a huge market, desperate for cloud computing on-premises.
The on-premises market was sick of being told that they either don't exist or that they don't deserve modernity.

The quickening pace always seemed to be due in some way to our transparency: new customers were listeners to our podcast, or they had read our RFDs, or they had perused our documentation, or they had looked at the source code itself.
We're going to kick butt, have fun, not cheat (of course!), love our customers—and change computing forever.
posts.key_insight: Radical transparency—through podcasts, public RFDs, documentation, and open source code—became Oxide's unexpected competitive advantage, dramatically shortening enterprise sales cycles by letting customers deeply evaluate the product before engaging.
