posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on the unique challenges of developing 'systems software in the large' - complex foundational software that combines the difficulty of low-level systems programming with the organizational challenges of building big software across many people over extended periods. He uses Oxide's software update system as a case study, describing it as a 'wicked problem' that requires updating a distributed system while maintaining operability during the transition, all while supporting air-gapped deployments without the crutch of manual operator intervention. The post celebrates Dave Pacheco's OxCon talk on leading this two-year project, praising both the technical achievement and the hard-won organizational wisdom shared.
key_points: Systems software provides abstractions that hide 'filthy details' - implementing those systems means embracing the gnarly implications
Programming in the large adds orthogonal challenges of scope, composition, and multi-person development over time
Wicked problems in software are not only never completely solved, but not even understood until implementation is well underway
Oxide's software update must work across air gaps without relying on 'the public cloud's hidden crutch of operators and runbooks'
Organizational challenges like 'organizational procrastination' and scope creep are as critical as technical ones
money_quotes: Systems software—the silent engine room of modern infrastructure—is especially so. By design, systems software provides an abstraction for programs, insulating programmers from the filthy details that lie beneath; piercing that abstraction to implement the underlying system is to embrace those details and their gnarly implications.
A system that is merely functional can be deceptively distant from the robustness required of foundational software.
Intersecting these two challenges—that is, developing systems software in the large—is to take on the most grueling of projects: it is the stuff of which mythical man months are literally made.
We cannot rely on the public cloud's hidden crutch of operators and runbooks. It is a problem so wicked, you can practically hear it cackle.
Should you be the kind of sicko inexplicably drawn to exactly the kind of nasty problems that Dave describes, consider joining us.
posts.key_insight: Building large-scale systems software compounds the already brutal challenges of low-level programming with organizational complexity, requiring not just technical excellence but hard-won wisdom about focus, scope, and team leadership.

posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill reflects on the Oakland Ballers' 2025 Pioneer League championship victory, weaving together his family's history as Oakland A's fans, the trauma of the A's relocation by owner John Fisher, and the redemptive emergence of this independent league team. The essay explores how sports teams become vessels for family memories across generations, and how the scrappy Ballers—staffed largely by local NorCal players—provided Oakland with an unexpected path to healing after losing their MLB franchise.
key_points: The Oakland Ballers won the 2025 Pioneer League championship, Oakland's first baseball title since 1989
The A's relocation by owner John Fisher traumatized generations of Oakland fans who had built family traditions around the team
The Ballers emerged in 2024 from the ashes of Oakland baseball, transforming historic Raimondi Park into a community gathering place
The 2025 roster featured many local NorCal players from regional colleges, creating deeper community connection
The team came back from 0-2 in the championship series to win three straight at home
money_quotes: The beauty of baseball is the narrative beyond the diamond.
To rip a team out of a city, then, is to sour some of our dearest memories, ones that cannot be recreated; it is to come into a home and desecrate the family photo album.
Anytime something ends, something else begins.
They slid in the outfield, making snow angels on the wet ground. It was everything great about sports, in a single joyous moment.
For all of them, wherever their careers may take them, they will forever remember that electric Sunday night in West Oakland, when they proved what is possible when we refuse to give up on ourselves.
posts.key_insight: Sports teams matter not for the entertainment they provide but for the generations of family memories they hold—and while a franchise can be stolen, a community's love of the game cannot be extinguished.

posts.summary: Cantrill argues that LinkedIn has become a viable social network by default as competitors self-destructed, but warns that the platform is being overrun by LLM-generated content that is painfully obvious to readers. He contends that using AI to write posts destroys authenticity and causes readers to disengage entirely, unable to distinguish real insights from 'generated fanfic.' While acknowledging LLMs are useful for brainstorming and editing, he insists they make terrible writers and urges people to trust their own voice.
key_points: LinkedIn succeeded by being the 'Gerald Ford' of social networks - boring but not corrupt
LLM-generated content has obvious stylistic tells that most readers now recognize
Using AI to write destroys the authenticity that makes personal perspectives valuable
LLMs are useful for brainstorming and editing but poor at original writing
Readers stop engaging when they can't tell if content is genuine
money_quotes: LinkedIn is the Gerald Ford of social networks: the normal one that was left standing as the Richard Nixons and the Spiro Agnews of social media imploded around them.
Bluntly, your intellectual fly is open: lots of people notice—but no one is pointing it out.
When you—person whose perspective I want to hear!—are obviously using an LLM to write posts for you, I don't know what's real and what is in fact generated fanfic.
LLMs are also lousy writers and (most importantly!) they are not you.
Have some confidence in your own voice—and write your own content.
posts.key_insight: LLM-generated content is instantly recognizable and destroys the authenticity that makes someone's perspective worth reading in the first place.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on how corporate disdain for customers drove him from Oracle after the Sun acquisition, using a recent conversation with a former colleague now at Broadcom to illustrate how this toxic attitude persists in tech acquisitions. The colleague's dismissive 'some that tried came crawling back lol' comment about VMware customers epitomizes the contempt Cantrill finds repugnant. He argues that companies built on customer coercion cannot sustain innovation and will not endure, contrasting this with Oxide's explicit mission to love its customers.
key_points: Customer contempt was the primary reason Cantrill left Oracle after Sun acquisition
Broadcom's VMware acquisition mirrors Oracle's customer-hostile approach
Being sued by customers is shameful and indicates a broken relationship
Coercion-based business models are antithetical to innovation
Enduring companies are built on genuine customer love, not lock-in
money_quotes: I could not stomach the disdain that Oracle had for its own customers.
That last sentence—'some that tried came crawling back lol'—made my stomach turn, reminding me viscerally of the contempt that Oracle had for its own customers that I had found so loathsome.
Suing your customers is gross—and being sued by your customers is shameful.
Companies that have disdain for their own customers will be reviled in return. Such companies may be able to thrive in the short term, but they do not endure in the limit.
When coercion is your business model, innovation is not merely unnecessary but actively antithetical.
We aspired to be 'the kind of company that customers will love to buy from—and employees will be proud to work for.'
posts.key_insight: Companies that treat customer lock-in as a business model rather than earning customer trust through innovation are building on a foundation that cannot endure.

posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on the paradox that while his career appears to have been characterized by perfect timing, it rarely felt that way in the moment. At each major career juncture—entering university during a recession, choosing software engineering amid predictions of offshoring, focusing on operating systems when Unix seemed dead, and founding Oxide when VCs dismissed hardware companies—conventional wisdom suggested he was making the wrong choice. His success came not from timing markets correctly but from having the conviction to pursue what he loved despite external skepticism.
key_points: Being at the 'right place at the right time' often only becomes visible in retrospect
Conventional wisdom at each career stage suggested his choices were wrong
Resilience and conviction matter more than timing the market
Following your passion provides solace even when the path is difficult
money_quotes: While this is objectively true at some level, it also gave me a double take: it rarely felt true at the time.

Ed Yourdon had just written The Decline and Fall of the American Programmer, which boldly told any young computer science student that they were wasting their time—that all programming jobs would be done by cheap labor abroad. This argument felt wrong, but I was too in love with computer science to be talked out of it anyway.
The conventional wisdom in the mid-1990s was that operating systems were done—that Unix was in decline and that the future clearly belonged to Microsoft.

Sand Hill investors told us that 'we only fund SaaS companies', that our $20M seed round was 'too big', that 'there is no market.' (And most absurdly: 'if this is such a good idea, why are you the only ones doing it?')

Six years later—with VMware customers wanting to get away from Broadcom and with frontier AI companies realizing that there is (in fact!) a lot of general purpose CPU involved in their workloads—our timing looks perfect, but in fact we are just resilient.
Have the courage to follow your heart—and disregard what others say about your timing.
posts.key_insight: What appears as perfect timing in hindsight is actually the result of having conviction in your work and resilience against conventional wisdom that tells you you're wrong.
